questions.prompt: Weak bond between water molecules responsible for cohesion and high specific heat.
questions.explanation: Hydrogen bonds form between the partially positive H of one water molecule and the partially negative O of another (bond energy ~20 kJ/mol vs. ~470 kJ/mol for the O-H covalent bond). Linus Pauling formalized the concept in 'The Nature of the Chemical Bond' (1939). H-bonds explain water's cohesion (transpiration in xylem), adhesion (capillary action), high specific heat (4.18 J/g·°C — stabilizes climate and cell temperature), and ice density anomaly (lakes freeze top-down). AP Bio Unit 1 frequently asks students to model how H-bonding underpins emergent properties — a Science Practice 1 (modeling) staple.

questions.prompt: Property of water that allows insects like water striders to walk on its surface.
questions.explanation: Surface tension arises because water molecules at the air-water interface H-bond inward and laterally but not upward, creating a film. Water's surface tension is 72.8 mN/m at 20°C — among the highest of common liquids. Water striders (Gerridae) exploit this with hydrophobic legs. AP Bio links this to xylem transport (Dixon-Joly cohesion-tension theory, 1894) and asks students to explain how cohesion+adhesion+tension move water 100+ m up a redwood without metabolic energy. Exam tip: distinguish cohesion (water-water) from adhesion (water-other).

questions.prompt: Bond linking two amino acids, formed by removal of water between -COOH and -NH2.
questions.explanation: Peptide bonds (C-N amide linkage) form during translation on the ribosome's peptidyl transferase center, which Cech and Steitz showed is ribozyme-catalyzed (rRNA, not protein). The bond has partial double-bond character making it planar and rigid, with cis/trans isomerism (Pro is exceptional). Sanger sequenced insulin (1955, Nobel 1958) by acid-hydrolyzing peptide bonds. AP Bio Unit 1 FRQs ask students to draw dehydration synthesis and identify the water lost — a Science Practice 1 modeling task. Hydrolysis (digestion) reverses this with pepsin (stomach pH 2) or trypsin (small intestine).

questions.prompt: PH unit measuring concentration of this ion, with each unit representing a 10-fold change.
questions.explanation: PH = -log[H+]. Sørensen introduced pH in 1909 at Carlsberg Laboratory. A pH of 5 has 10× more H+ than pH 6 and 100× more than pH 7. Blood pH is tightly buffered at 7.35-7.45 by the bicarbonate system (CO2 + H2O ⇌ H2CO3 ⇌ HCO3- + H+); deviations cause acidosis or alkalosis. AP Bio Unit 1 commonly presents pH graphs and asks students to calculate H+ ratios — a Science Practice 5 (data analysis) skill. Ocean acidification (pH dropped from 8.2 to 8.1 since 1850) is a frequent FRQ context.

questions.prompt: Anomalous density behavior of water at 4°C, critical for lake ecology in winter.
questions.explanation: Liquid water reaches maximum density at 3.98°C (1.000 g/mL); below this, H-bonds lock molecules into the open hexagonal crystal of ice (Ih), making ice ~9% less dense (0.917 g/mL). This means lakes freeze top-down — fish, plants, and microbes survive under an insulating ice layer. Without this anomaly, lakes would freeze solid bottom-up and aquatic ecosystems above 40° latitude could not exist. AP Bio Unit 1 ties this to climate regulation and asks students to model the structural cause (4 H-bonds per H2O in ice vs. ~3.4 in liquid).

questions.prompt: Sugar in DNA, lacking the 2' hydroxyl found on its RNA counterpart.
questions.explanation: 2'-deoxyribose is the 5-carbon sugar in DNA; removing the 2' -OH makes DNA chemically more stable (no intramolecular attack on the phosphodiester bond) — a reason DNA stores information for billions of years while RNA degrades in minutes. Ribose's 2'-OH allows RNA to fold into catalytic shapes (ribozymes — Cech and Altman, Nobel 1989). Reduction at the 2' position is performed by ribonucleotide reductase (RNR), which generates the deoxyribonucleotide pool for replication. AP Bio Unit 6 expects students to distinguish DNA vs. RNA chemistry, stability, and function.

questions.prompt: Pairing rule where adenine binds thymine with two H-bonds and guanine binds cytosine with three.
questions.explanation: Chargaff (1950) discovered %A = %T and %G = %C in DNA — a clue Watson and Crick used in 1953 to build the antiparallel double helix (with Franklin's X-ray photo 51 and Wilkins' data). A:T = 2 H-bonds; G:C = 3 H-bonds (GC-rich DNA has higher melting temp). The purines (A, G — two rings) always pair with pyrimidines (C, T, U — one ring), keeping the helix uniform at ~20 Å diameter. AP Bio Unit 6 FRQs use %A to calculate %G, etc., and to predict melting temperature differences.

questions.prompt: Substance that resists pH changes by accepting or donating protons.
questions.explanation: A buffer is a weak acid/conjugate base pair (e.g., H2CO3/HCO3-, H2PO4-/HPO4^2-) that neutralizes added H+ or OH-. The Henderson-Hasselbalch equation (pH = pKa + log[A-]/[HA], Henderson 1908, Hasselbalch 1916) predicts buffering capacity. Blood's bicarbonate buffer (pKa 6.1) keeps pH 7.4 despite CO2 production from cellular respiration. AP Bio FRQs often give a CO2-rising scenario and ask students to predict pH shifts and physiological responses (faster breathing to expel CO2). Carbonic anhydrase catalyzes the rate-limiting step.

questions.prompt: Storage polysaccharide of plants, made of α-1,4-linked glucose with α-1,6 branches.
questions.explanation: Starch comes in two forms: amylose (unbranched α-1,4 helix) and amylopectin (α-1,6 branches every ~24-30 glucoses). Synthesized in chloroplast stroma and amyloplasts, then hydrolyzed by α-amylase (saliva, pancreas) and β-amylase. Contrast with glycogen (animal storage, more branched, every ~8-12 glucoses, in liver/muscle cytoplasm as granules) and cellulose (β-1,4 glucose — structural, indigestible to most animals without microbes). Iodine-KI test stains amylose blue-black, a classic AP lab. AP Bio Unit 1 expects students to link branching pattern to rapid mobilization for energy.

questions.prompt: Meselson and Stahl proved DNA replicates by this mechanism, not conservative.
questions.explanation: Meselson and Stahl (1958) grew E. coli on 15N-NH4Cl (heavy nitrogen), shifted to 14N, and used CsCl density gradient centrifugation. After one generation, all DNA was hybrid density (one heavy + one light strand) — ruling out conservative replication; after two generations, half hybrid, half light — confirming semiconservative replication, as Watson and Crick predicted (1953). 'The most beautiful experiment in biology' (John Cairns). AP Bio Unit 6 expects students to interpret CsCl band positions and rule out conservative and dispersive models.

questions.prompt: Local folded segments stabilized by H-bonds between backbone amide groups.
questions.explanation: Secondary (2°) structures — α-helices (3.6 residues/turn, 5.4 Å pitch) and β-pleated sheets — were predicted by Pauling and Corey (1951) from X-ray data and stabilized by backbone N-H···O=C H-bonds spaced every 4th residue (α) or between parallel/antiparallel strands (β). Pro disrupts α-helices (kink); Gly is the most flexible. Ramachandran plots (1963) map allowed φ/ψ angles. AP Bio Unit 1 expects students to recognize that R-group identity influences but does not directly form 2° structure — the backbone does. β-keratin (silk) is mostly β-sheet; α-keratin (hair, nails) is α-helix coiled-coils.

questions.prompt: 3D folded shape of a single polypeptide, stabilized by R-group interactions and disulfides.
questions.explanation: Tertiary (3°) structure emerges from R-group interactions: hydrophobic core packing (entropy-driven by water exclusion), H-bonds, ionic bridges (e.g., Lys+···Glu-), van der Waals contacts, and covalent disulfide bonds (Cys-S-S-Cys) in oxidizing environments like the ER lumen and extracellular space. Hydrophobic effect (Kauzmann 1959) is the major driving force. Misfolded 3° structures cause amyloid diseases (Alzheimer's β-amyloid, Parkinson's α-synuclein, prions — Prusiner 1982, Nobel 1997). AP Bio Unit 1 uses denaturation experiments (heat, pH, urea) and renaturation as a recurring FRQ setup.

questions.prompt: Quaternary assembly of four subunits in this oxygen-transport protein.
questions.explanation: Hemoglobin is α2β2 — two α-globins (141 aa) and two β-globins (146 aa), each cradling a heme group (Fe2+ porphyrin) for O2 binding (Perutz 1959 X-ray structure, Nobel 1962). Cooperative binding (Hill coefficient ≈ 2.8) yields a sigmoidal O2-dissociation curve, allowing efficient O2 pickup in lungs (PO2 100 mmHg, 98% saturation) and unloading in tissues (PO2 40 mmHg, 75%). Bohr effect: ↑CO2/↓pH right-shifts the curve, releasing more O2 to active tissues. Sickle-cell (HbS) and thalassemia are AP Bio's go-to Unit 1+5+6 case studies linking 1° → 4° → phenotype.

questions.prompt: Process where heat, pH, or chaotropes unfold proteins, destroying function.
questions.explanation: Denaturation disrupts noncovalent interactions (H-bonds, hydrophobic packing, ionic bridges) and sometimes disulfides (with reducing agents like β-mercaptoethanol). Anfinsen's classic experiment: ribonuclease A denatured in 8 M urea + βME and renatured to active form on agent removal — proving primary structure encodes the fold. Heat (raw → cooked egg white = ovalbumin denaturation), extreme pH (acid → milk curdling), heavy metals, and detergents (SDS in PAGE) all denature. AP Bio Unit 1 FRQs ask students to interpret enzyme activity vs. temperature/pH curves — bell-shaped peak at optimum, denaturation past it.

questions.prompt: Inhibitor binding a site away from the active site, often with sigmoidal kinetics.
questions.explanation: Allosteric regulators bind a site other than the active site (Greek allos = other, stereos = solid) and shift the enzyme between T (tense, low-activity) and R (relaxed, high-activity) states (Monod-Wyman-Changeux 1965 MWC model; Koshland-Némethy-Filmer 1966 KNF). Phosphofructokinase-1 (PFK-1) is the textbook example: ATP and citrate inhibit (negative feedback when energy is plentiful); AMP and F-2,6-BP activate. ATCase (Lipscomb 1968 structure) shows the canonical R↔T transition. End-product inhibition (Pardee 1956 ATCase by CTP) is a recurring FRQ topic.

questions.prompt: Biological catalyst that lowers activation energy without being consumed.
questions.explanation: Enzymes are typically globular proteins (some are ribozymes — Cech and Altman 1980s, Nobel 1989) that lower activation energy (Ea) by stabilizing the transition state. They do NOT change ΔG or equilibrium — only kinetics. Specificity arises from the active site's shape and chemistry (induced fit, Koshland 1958, refining Fischer's 1894 lock-and-key). Names usually end in -ase: lactase, DNA polymerase, ATP synthase. Turnover numbers range from 1 (lysozyme) to 600,000/s (catalase). AP Bio Unit 1 and 3 FRQs require students to graph reaction rate vs. substrate, temperature, and pH and identify Vmax, Km, and denaturation thresholds.

questions.prompt: Region of an enzyme where substrate binds and chemistry occurs.
questions.explanation: The active site is a 3D cleft (often <5% of enzyme volume) lined with catalytic residues (e.g., Ser-His-Asp catalytic triad in serine proteases like chymotrypsin and trypsin — Blow 1969). Induced fit means substrate binding triggers conformational change that optimizes catalysis (Koshland 1958). Hexokinase clamps onto glucose like a hand around a ball. Mutations to active-site residues abolish activity — a common AP Bio Unit 1 FRQ setup using site-directed mutagenesis data (Smith 1978, Nobel 1993). Allosteric sites are separate regulatory pockets; not the active site.

questions.prompt: Substrate concentration producing half of Vmax — measure of enzyme-substrate affinity.
questions.explanation: Michaelis and Menten (1913) derived V = Vmax[S]/(Km+[S]). Km has units of concentration (mM, μM) and equals [S] at V = Vmax/2. Low Km = high affinity. Hexokinase (Km ≈ 0.1 mM glucose) saturates at normal blood glucose; glucokinase (Km ≈ 10 mM, liver) responds proportionally to glucose surges after meals. Lineweaver-Burk double-reciprocal plots linearize the hyperbola: 1/V vs 1/[S], y-intercept = 1/Vmax, x-intercept = -1/Km. AP Bio Unit 3 expects competitive inhibition (↑Km, same Vmax) vs. noncompetitive (same Km, ↓Vmax) graph reading.

questions.prompt: Inhibitor that binds the active site, mimicking substrate; reversed by more substrate.
questions.explanation: Competitive inhibitors resemble substrate and compete for the active site, increasing apparent Km without changing Vmax (saturation overcomes inhibition). Methotrexate inhibits dihydrofolate reductase (DHFR) by mimicking folate — a chemotherapy mainstay since 1947 (Sidney Farber). Statins competitively inhibit HMG-CoA reductase, lowering cholesterol. Sulfanilamide mimics PABA, blocking bacterial folate synthesis (Domagk 1932, Nobel 1939). AP Bio Unit 3 FRQs give Lineweaver-Burk plots with two lines crossing the y-axis (competitive) vs. parallel or crossing elsewhere (noncompetitive) and ask students to classify.

questions.prompt: Term describing the opposite orientation of the two DNA strands.
questions.explanation: DNA's two strands run antiparallel: one 5'→3', the other 3'→5'. This is a thermodynamic and steric consequence of B-form geometry (Watson and Crick 1953). Antiparallel orientation is why DNA polymerase, which only adds to 3'-OH, must use a continuous leading strand and a discontinuous lagging strand (Okazaki fragments, 1968). Antiparallel β-sheets in proteins are H-bond stabilized similarly. AP Bio Unit 6 FRQs require students to draw a replication fork with all four enzymes (helicase, primase, DNA pol III, ligase) and label 5'/3' ends correctly.

questions.prompt: Monomer of proteins, joined by peptide bonds via dehydration synthesis.
questions.explanation: The 20 proteinogenic amino acids share a central α-carbon bonded to an amine (-NH2), carboxyl (-COOH), H, and variable R group. R groups dictate properties: hydrophobic (Leu, Val), hydrophilic (Ser, Thr), charged (Lys+, Glu-), special (Pro kinks, Cys disulfides, Gly flexibility). Stanley Miller's 1953 spark-chamber experiment (Urey lab) produced glycine and alanine abiotically. AP Bio Unit 1 expects students to read R-group tables and predict folding/function consequences. The genetic code (Nirenberg 1961-66) maps 64 codons to these 20 amino acids.

questions.prompt: Linear sequence of amino acids determined by mRNA codons.
questions.explanation: Primary (1°) structure is the covalent sequence of amino acids, dictated by gene → mRNA → ribosomal translation. Anfinsen's 1957-1962 ribonuclease refolding experiments (Nobel 1972) proved that 1° structure determines all higher structure under physiological conditions — the 'Anfinsen dogma.' Sickle-cell anemia is the classic AP example: a single Glu6→Val6 substitution in β-globin (HbA→HbS) causes deoxygenated hemoglobin to polymerize and sickle red cells (Ingram 1957). AP Bio Unit 1 FRQs ask students to predict structural and functional consequences of point mutations — Science Practice 6 (argumentation).

questions.prompt: Bond linking the 3' carbon of one nucleotide to the 5' carbon of the next.
questions.explanation: Phosphodiester bonds form between the 3'-OH of one nucleotide's sugar and the 5'-phosphate of the next, releasing pyrophosphate (PPi) when DNA polymerase joins incoming dNTPs. This 5'→3' directionality is rigorously preserved: polymerases ONLY add to the 3' end. Hydrolysis of PPi by pyrophosphatase makes the reaction effectively irreversible (drives equilibrium). AP Bio Unit 6 FRQs commonly draw nucleotide structures and ask students to mark 5', 3', phosphates, and sugars. Mismatches in phosphodiester formation are corrected by proofreading 3'→5' exonuclease activity.

questions.prompt: Lipid with three fatty acids esterified to glycerol; main energy storage form.
questions.explanation: Triglycerides store ~9 kcal/g vs. 4 kcal/g for carbs — making fat the densest cellular fuel. Synthesis (lipogenesis) in liver/adipose uses glycerol-3-phosphate + fatty acyl-CoA; breakdown (lipolysis) by hormone-sensitive lipase yields free fatty acids that enter β-oxidation, generating acetyl-CoA for the citric acid cycle. Saturated (no C=C) vs. unsaturated (cis/trans C=C) differ in melting points and health effects — trans fats from partial hydrogenation are FDA-banned (2018). AP Bio Unit 1 expects students to compare energy storage in plants (starch) vs. animals (glycogen + triglycerides) and link to Unit 3 cellular respiration.

questions.prompt: Nucleic acid level where double helix coils around histone octamers into nucleosomes.
questions.explanation: DNA wraps 1.65 turns (147 bp) around an octamer of histones H2A, H2B, H3, H4 (two of each) to form the nucleosome — the 'beads on a string' Roger Kornberg discovered (1974, Nobel 2006). H1 linker histone clamps the wrapped DNA. Higher-order folding creates the 30 nm fiber, then loops anchored to a scaffold, then chromosomes during M phase (1:10,000 compaction). Histone tail modifications (acetylation→active, methylation→variable) regulate gene expression (Unit 6). AP Bio Unit 1 and 6 link chromatin structure to transcriptional accessibility.

questions.prompt: Organelle nicknamed the 'powerhouse,' with its own circular DNA and 70S ribosomes.
questions.explanation: Mitochondria (Altmann 1890 'bioblasts'; Benda 1898 named them) are 0.5-1 μm double-membrane organelles where oxidative phosphorylation (Unit 3) produces ~30 ATP per glucose. They have their own ~16.5 kb circular genome (37 genes, all maternal inheritance — Wallace 1980), 70S bacterial-like ribosomes, and divide by binary fission. Lynn Margulis (1967) proposed the endosymbiotic theory: ancestral α-proteobacteria engulfed by archaeal hosts ~2 Bya. AP Bio Unit 2 + 7 expect students to cite four endosymbiosis evidence lines: double membrane, own DNA, own ribosomes, binary fission.

questions.prompt: Domain of life that includes bacteria and archaea — cells lacking a nucleus.
questions.explanation: Prokaryotes (Greek: pro = before, karyon = nucleus) lack membrane-bound organelles. They have circular DNA in a nucleoid, 70S ribosomes (50S+30S subunits — target of streptomycin, tetracycline, erythromycin), cell walls (peptidoglycan in Bacteria, pseudomurein/S-layers in Archaea), and reproduce by binary fission. Woese (1977) split prokaryotes into Bacteria and Archaea based on 16S rRNA — three-domain tree of life (Bacteria, Archaea, Eukarya). AP Bio Unit 2 + 7 expect students to know prokaryotic features and the endosymbiotic origin of eukaryotic organelles.

questions.prompt: Plant/algal organelle that contains chlorophyll, thylakoids, and stroma.
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts (3-10 μm) house photosynthesis (Unit 3). Their double membrane plus internal thylakoid membranes (stacked into grana) contain chlorophyll a/b, accessory pigments, and the photosystem II–b6f–photosystem I electron transport chain. The stroma holds the Calvin cycle's RuBisCO (most abundant protein on Earth). Endosymbiotic origin: cyanobacteria-like ancestor engulfed ~1.5-2 Bya (primary endosymbiosis). Their 120-160 kb circular DNA encodes ~100 genes; the rest moved to the nucleus. AP Bio Unit 2 FRQs require students to map photosynthesis locations (thylakoid lumen vs. stroma) — a Science Practice 1 modeling skill.

questions.prompt: Cell type with membrane-bound organelles and a true nucleus.
questions.explanation: Eukaryotes (true nucleus) emerged ~2 Bya, likely via archaeal-host + bacterial-endosymbiont fusion (Lokiarchaeota lineage — Spang 2015, supporting the Asgard hypothesis). They have linear chromosomes, 80S ribosomes (60S+40S), membrane-bound organelles (nucleus, ER, Golgi, mitochondria, lysosomes, plus chloroplasts in plants/algae), and a cytoskeleton (actin, microtubules, intermediate filaments). Multicellularity arose independently in animals, plants, fungi, and several protist lineages. AP Bio Unit 2 + 7 ask students to identify cellular features that support endosymbiosis and to construct phylogenetic arguments using rRNA data.

questions.prompt: Organelle containing hydrolytic enzymes active at acidic pH for cellular digestion.
questions.explanation: Lysosomes (de Duve 1955, Nobel 1974) are single-membrane organelles maintaining internal pH ~4.5 via V-type H+-ATPases. They contain ~50 acid hydrolases (proteases, nucleases, lipases, glycosidases) that degrade endocytosed material, defective organelles (autophagy — Ohsumi 2016 Nobel), and engulfed bacteria. Lysosomal storage diseases include Tay-Sachs (HEXA deficiency, ganglioside accumulation), Gaucher (β-glucocerebrosidase), Pompe (acid α-glucosidase). Plant cells lack lysosomes per se; the central vacuole serves a similar role. AP Bio Unit 2 connects lysosomes to autophagy, programmed cell death, and macrophage function in immunity.

questions.prompt: Bilayer molecule with hydrophilic head and two hydrophobic tails forming the membrane.
questions.explanation: Phospholipids have a glycerol backbone, two fatty acid tails, and a phosphate-linked polar head group (choline → phosphatidylcholine, ethanolamine → PE, serine → PS, inositol → PI). The amphipathic nature drives spontaneous bilayer assembly (hydrophobic effect — Tanford 1973). Bilayers are ~5 nm thick. Asymmetry: PS faces cytoplasm; flipped to outer leaflet during apoptosis as 'eat me' signal for macrophages. AP Bio Unit 2 expects students to draw a bilayer with heads/tails and identify polar vs. nonpolar regions — a Science Practice 1 modeling skill.

questions.prompt: Eukaryotic structure separating DNA from translation, allowing complex regulation.
questions.explanation: The nucleus (5-10 μm) is bounded by a double membrane (nuclear envelope) continuous with the rough ER. Nuclear pores (NPCs — ~30 protein nucleoporins, 120 MDa) gate molecular traffic: <40 kDa diffuses freely; larger cargo needs Ran-GTP-driven import via importin α/β (recognizing NLS sequences). Within: chromatin (DNA + histones), the nucleolus (rRNA transcription and 60S/40S subunit assembly), and the nuclear lamina (intermediate filaments — lamin A mutations cause Hutchinson-Gilford progeria). AP Bio Unit 2 + 6 expect students to link nuclear separation to post-transcriptional mRNA processing (capping, splicing, polyA — Unit 6).

questions.prompt: Organelle that synthesizes secreted and membrane proteins on bound ribosomes.
questions.explanation: Rough ER is studded with ribosomes synthesizing proteins destined for secretion, membranes, or lysosomes. Signal-recognition particle (SRP — Blobel 1975, Nobel 1999) recognizes the N-terminal signal peptide and docks the ribosome to the Sec61 translocon for co-translational import. Inside the lumen, chaperones (BiP/GRP78) and disulfide isomerase (PDI) fold the nascent chain; glycosylation begins. Misfolded proteins trigger the unfolded protein response (UPR) and ERAD degradation. AP Bio Unit 2 emphasizes the endomembrane pathway: rough ER → COPII vesicles → cis-Golgi → trans-Golgi → secretory vesicle → plasma membrane.

questions.prompt: Membrane lipid that buffers fluidity across temperatures and is found in animal cells.
questions.explanation: Cholesterol's rigid four-ring sterol nucleus + hydroxyl head + isooctyl tail wedges between phospholipids. At high temperatures it restrains motion (decreases fluidity); at low temperatures it prevents tight packing (maintains fluidity) — a 'fluidity buffer.' Animal cells contain ~30% of membrane lipid as cholesterol; plants use phytosterols (sitosterol, stigmasterol); fungi use ergosterol (target of antifungal azoles). Cholesterol also organizes lipid rafts — Tdm of sphingolipid/cholesterol patches that platform signaling proteins. AP Bio Unit 2 FRQs commonly test cold-acclimation responses (more unsaturation + cholesterol adjustments).

questions.prompt: Stack of flattened cisternae that modifies, sorts, and packages proteins.
questions.explanation: Discovered by Camillo Golgi (1898, Nobel 1906), the Golgi consists of cis-, medial-, and trans-cisternae. Cargo arrives at cis-face in COPII vesicles, transits via cisternal maturation (Bonifacino, Glick), is modified (sugar trimming, sulfation, phosphorylation — mannose-6-phosphate tags lysosomal enzymes), and exits trans-face in clathrin-coated vesicles (lysosomal) or secretory vesicles (regulated/constitutive). I-cell disease (mucolipidosis II) shows what happens when M6P tagging fails — lysosomal enzymes are missecreted. AP Bio Unit 2 FRQs trace a secreted protein from nucleus → mRNA → ribosome → rough ER → Golgi → vesicle → membrane fusion.

questions.prompt: Passive movement of molecules across a membrane down their concentration gradient.
questions.explanation: Simple diffusion (Fick's law: J = -D·dC/dx) is the spontaneous net movement of small nonpolar molecules (O2, CO2, N2, steroid hormones) across the bilayer down their concentration/partial-pressure gradient — no protein, no ATP. Permeability scales with lipid solubility (Overton's rule, 1899) and inversely with size. AP Bio Unit 2 expects students to predict which substances cross by simple diffusion (small, nonpolar) vs. need transporters (ions, polar molecules, large molecules). The lab classic: comparing dialysis tubing solute movement to demonstrate diffusion ≠ active.

questions.prompt: Transport through a channel or carrier protein, down gradient, no ATP.
questions.explanation: Facilitated diffusion uses channels (fast, gated by voltage/ligand/mechanical force) or carriers (slower, alternating-access conformational change) to move ions/polar molecules down their gradient. Glucose enters cells via GLUT transporters (GLUT1 in RBCs, GLUT4 insulin-regulated in muscle/fat). Aquaporins facilitate water 10⁹/s per pore. K+ leak channels set the resting membrane potential. AP Bio Unit 2 FRQs distinguish facilitated (saturable, follows Michaelis-Menten kinetics) from simple diffusion (linear, never saturates) — graph reading question.

questions.prompt: Transport using ATP to pump ions against their gradient; sets up Na+/K+ resting potential.
questions.explanation: Active transport uses ATP hydrolysis (primary) or ion-gradient coupling (secondary, e.g., Na+/glucose SGLT cotransporter in gut) to move solutes against gradients. The Na+/K+ ATPase (Skou 1957, Nobel 1997) pumps 3 Na+ out and 2 K+ in per ATP, maintaining the resting membrane potential (~-70 mV) and the gradient that powers nerve impulses (Unit 4 signaling) and secondary transporters. Other examples: Ca2+ ATPase (SERCA in ER), H+/K+ ATPase (stomach parietal cells, target of omeprazole). AP Bio Unit 2 FRQs test the 4-state alternating-access cycle and ATP consumption rates.

questions.prompt: Type of protein spanning the entire bilayer with α-helical or β-barrel transmembrane domains.
questions.explanation: Integral (transmembrane) proteins span the lipid bilayer with α-helices (most — typically 20-25 hydrophobic residues) or β-barrels (in porins like bacterial OmpF and mitochondrial outer membrane). They function as transporters (Na+/K+ ATPase, GLUT4 — 12 TM helices), channels (aquaporins, K+ channels — MacKinnon 2003 Nobel for KcsA), receptors (GPCRs — 7 TM helices, Lefkowitz/Kobilka 2012 Nobel), and adhesion molecules (integrins, cadherins). Extraction requires detergents (SDS, Triton X-100). Peripheral proteins, by contrast, attach by electrostatic/H-bond to membrane surface and wash off with salt or pH change.

questions.prompt: Bulk transport of large materials via vesicle budding and fusion, in or out of the cell.
questions.explanation: Endocytosis ingests material via membrane invagination: phagocytosis (cell eating — macrophages engulfing bacteria), pinocytosis (cell drinking — non-specific), and receptor-mediated (clathrin-coated pits, e.g., LDL receptor in cholesterol uptake — Brown and Goldstein 1985 Nobel; familial hypercholesterolemia results from LDLR mutations). Exocytosis fuses secretory vesicles to the plasma membrane via SNARE proteins (Rothman, Schekman, Südhof 2013 Nobel); regulated exocytosis releases neurotransmitters (synaptic vesicles, ~0.5 ms Ca2+-triggered fusion) and hormones (insulin from β-cells). AP Bio Unit 2 + 4 link exocytosis to signal release.

questions.prompt: Ratio that decreases as cells grow, limiting maximum cell size.
questions.explanation: As a cell grows, volume increases as r³ but surface area as r² — so SA:V drops. Since nutrients enter and wastes leave through the surface, large cells can't supply or clear fast enough. This drives cell division (Unit 4) and explains why metabolically active cells (neurons with long thin axons; intestinal epithelia with microvilli; alveoli with foldings) maximize SA:V. Microvilli increase intestinal absorptive surface ~25×. AP Bio Unit 2 FRQ: given a sphere or cube grid, calculate SA:V at different sizes — pure Science Practice 5 quantitative reasoning.

questions.prompt: Singer-Nicolson model name for the membrane's dynamic, lateral protein distribution.
questions.explanation: Singer and Nicolson (1972, Science) proposed the fluid mosaic model after Frye-Edidin's 1970 mouse-human cell fusion experiment showed surface antigens intermix within 40 min — proof membranes are 2D fluids. Proteins float laterally (~1 μm²/s diffusion) and rotate, but rarely flip across the bilayer. Cholesterol modulates fluidity: at low T, prevents tight packing (keeps fluid); at high T, stiffens membrane. AP Bio Unit 2 expects students to predict fluidity changes with temperature and lipid composition (more unsaturated → more fluid, longer chains → less fluid).

questions.prompt: Finger-like membrane projections on intestinal cells that increase absorption surface.
questions.explanation: Microvilli (~1 μm long, 0.1 μm wide) cover the apical surface of enterocytes (gut), kidney proximal tubule cells, and many epithelia, forming the 'brush border.' Each is supported by ~20-30 parallel actin filaments cross-linked by villin and fimbrin. Brush-border enzymes (sucrase-isomaltase, lactase, peptidases) digest sugars and peptides at the surface. AP Bio Unit 2 contrasts microvilli (passive surface amplifiers) with cilia/flagella (motile, microtubule-based). Loss of microvilli (microvillus inclusion disease) causes severe malabsorption — a clinical anchor for the SA:V concept.

questions.prompt: Carbohydrate-protein attachment on cell surface used for recognition; ABO blood types.
questions.explanation: Glycoproteins have oligosaccharide chains covalently linked to Asn (N-linked) or Ser/Thr (O-linked) residues. They form the glycocalyx — a sugar coat on the cell surface critical for cell-cell recognition, immunity (ABO antigens differ by terminal sugars: A=GalNAc, B=Gal, O=H stem only), and pathogen binding (influenza hemagglutinin binds sialic acid). Hartwell, Hunt, and Nurse (Nobel 2001) used glycoprotein receptors as cell-cycle markers. AP Bio Unit 2 + 4 expect students to link glycoproteins to receptor function (Unit 4 signaling) and self/non-self recognition (immune response).

questions.prompt: Microtubule organizing center near the nucleus, containing two centrioles in animal cells.
questions.explanation: The centrosome organizes microtubules via γ-tubulin ring complexes (γ-TuRC) and contains two perpendicular centrioles (each is a 9-triplet microtubule barrel). In animal cells, the centrosome nucleates the mitotic spindle (Unit 4). Plant cells lack centrioles but still organize spindles. Centrosome amplification (extra centrosomes) is a hallmark of cancer cells (causing chromosome missegregation; Boveri 1914 first hypothesized the link). AP Bio Unit 2 FRQs occasionally ask students to predict mitotic outcomes when centrosome duplication is dysregulated.

questions.prompt: Diffusion of water across a selectively permeable membrane.
questions.explanation: Osmosis is water movement from low solute (hypotonic) → high solute (hypertonic) via aquaporins (Agre 2003 Nobel) and to a lesser extent through the bilayer. Osmotic pressure π = iMRT (van't Hoff equation). Tonicity terms: isotonic (no net flow), hypotonic (cell swells, may lyse), hypertonic (cell shrinks/crenates; plants plasmolyze). Plant cells use turgor pressure for rigidity — wilting = loss of turgor. AP Bio Unit 2's signature lab: potato cores or dialysis tubing in sucrose gradients, calculating water potential Ψ = Ψs + Ψp. Students must plot percent mass change vs. molarity to find isotonic point — a Science Practice 5 (data analysis) staple.

questions.prompt: Eukaryotic 9+2 microtubule arrangement powering swimming sperm and tracheal sweeping.
questions.explanation: Eukaryotic cilia/flagella share the 9+2 axoneme: 9 doublet microtubules around a central pair, with dynein motor arms generating sliding for bending (Gibbons 1965). Cilia are short and numerous (tracheal epithelium clears mucus; oviduct moves egg); flagella are long and few (sperm tail). Disorders include Kartagener's syndrome (primary ciliary dyskinesia — situs inversus, infertility, bronchiectasis from dynein mutations). Note: prokaryotic flagella have completely different structure (flagellin protein, proton-driven rotation — Berg 1973). AP Bio Unit 2 expects students to distinguish these two unrelated motility systems.

questions.prompt: Term for organelle's role in segregating reactions to optimize efficiency.
questions.explanation: Compartmentalization separates incompatible reactions (e.g., lysosomal pH 4.5 vs. cytoplasmic pH 7.2), concentrates enzymes with substrates (lysosomal hydrolases, mitochondrial citric acid cycle), and provides surface area for membrane reactions (cristae for ETC, thylakoids for light reactions). Lehninger and Kennedy (1948) located oxidative phosphorylation to mitochondria — establishing compartmentalization as a key cell-biology principle. AP Bio Unit 2 FRQs ask students to predict outcomes when compartmentalization fails (lysosomal storage diseases, ER stress, mitochondrial dysfunction in aging).

questions.prompt: Bacterial cell wall component made of peptidoglycan; target of penicillin.
questions.explanation: Peptidoglycan is a mesh of NAG-NAM (N-acetylglucosamine — N-acetylmuramic acid) chains cross-linked by short peptides (D-amino acids). Gram-positive bacteria have thick peptidoglycan (20-80 nm, retains crystal violet); Gram-negative have thin peptidoglycan (~2 nm) plus an outer LPS membrane. Penicillin (Fleming 1928, Florey/Chain 1940 mass production) blocks transpeptidase (penicillin-binding proteins), preventing cross-link formation; nascent walls rupture from turgor. Lysozyme (in tears, saliva) hydrolyzes NAG-NAM bonds. AP Bio Unit 2 contrasts plant cell walls (cellulose β-1,4-glucan), fungal walls (chitin), and bacterial walls (peptidoglycan).

questions.prompt: Cytoplasmic protein network that maintains shape, enables movement, and positions organelles.
questions.explanation: The cytoskeleton has three filament systems: (1) microfilaments (actin, 7 nm — muscle contraction with myosin, cell crawling via lamellipodia, cytokinesis contractile ring); (2) intermediate filaments (8-12 nm — keratins in epithelia, vimentin in mesenchyme, lamins in nucleus — most stable, provide mechanical strength); (3) microtubules (αβ-tubulin, 25 nm — mitotic spindle, intracellular transport via kinesin/dynein, cilia/flagella). Drugs target each: cytochalasin D (actin), paclitaxel/Taxol (stabilizes MTs — chemotherapy), colchicine (depolymerizes MTs — gout). AP Bio Unit 2 + 4 link cytoskeleton to mitosis, intracellular trafficking, and cell signaling.

questions.prompt: Universal energy currency molecule with high-energy phosphoanhydride bonds.
questions.explanation: ATP (adenosine triphosphate) has a high-energy γ-phosphate bond (ΔG° ≈ -30.5 kJ/mol on hydrolysis to ADP+Pi; closer to -50 to -57 kJ/mol in cells due to cellular [ATP]/[ADP][Pi] ratio). Lipmann (1941) proposed ATP as the energy currency; Lohmann discovered it in 1929. Cells turn over ~50 kg of ATP/day (body weight equivalent!) but only have ~50 g at any moment. AP Bio Unit 3 expects students to explain coupling: ATP-hydrolysis-released energy drives endergonic reactions like Na+/K+ pumping, muscle contraction (myosin), and biosynthesis. ATP synthase (Unit 3) is the regenerator.

questions.prompt: Second law thermodynamic measure of disorder; always increases in spontaneous processes.
questions.explanation: Entropy (S) measures the number of microstates a system can occupy: ΔS_universe > 0 for spontaneous processes (Second Law). Living cells appear to defy this by creating order — but they export entropy as heat and disordered metabolic waste, keeping ΔS_universe positive (Schrödinger 1944 'What is Life?'). Gibbs free energy: ΔG = ΔH - TΔS. Spontaneous (exergonic): ΔG < 0; nonspontaneous (endergonic): ΔG > 0. AP Bio Unit 3 FRQs use entropy to explain why protein folding (locally decreasing entropy) is offset by released water entropy (hydrophobic effect).

questions.prompt: Mitchell's mechanism: H+ gradient across the thylakoid membrane drives ATP synthesis.
questions.explanation: Peter Mitchell (1961 hypothesis, 1978 Nobel) proposed chemiosmotic coupling: the ETC pumps H+ across a membrane (thylakoid in chloroplasts, inner mitochondrial in respiration), creating a proton motive force (ΔμH+ = ΔpH + Δψ). H+ flows back through ATP synthase (F0F1), rotating its γ-subunit and driving ATP synthesis via Boyer's binding-change mechanism (1997 Nobel with Walker). In chloroplasts, the thylakoid lumen acidifies (pH ~5) vs. stroma (pH ~8) — a 1000× H+ gradient. Disrupting this with uncouplers (DNP — diet drug of the 1930s, lethal) collapses the gradient and stops ATP synthesis. AP Bio Unit 3 expects deep mastery of this mechanism.

questions.prompt: Inner mitochondrial membrane series of complexes pumping H+ and reducing O2 to H2O.
questions.explanation: The ETC consists of four complexes embedded in the inner mitochondrial membrane: Complex I (NADH dehydrogenase, NADH → ubiquinone, pumps 4 H+), Complex II (succinate dehydrogenase, FADH2 → ubiquinone, no H+ pump), Complex III (ubiquinone → cytochrome c, pumps 4 H+), Complex IV (cytochrome c oxidase, reduces O2 → H2O, pumps 2 H+). Each NADH yields ~2.5 ATP, each FADH2 ~1.5 ATP. Per glucose: ~30-32 ATP total. Mitchell's chemiosmotic theory (1978 Nobel) ties H+ gradient to ATP synthesis at ATP synthase (Complex V). Poisons: cyanide and CO inhibit Complex IV; rotenone Complex I; antimycin Complex III. AP Bio Unit 3 FRQs use poison data to deduce complex identity.

questions.prompt: Boyer's mechanism of ATP synthase using rotational catalysis.
questions.explanation: Paul Boyer (1997 Nobel with John Walker) proposed the binding-change mechanism for ATP synthase: the F0 motor (membrane-embedded c-ring) rotates with H+ flow, turning the γ-subunit, which sequentially shifts F1's three β-subunits through three states — open (releases ATP), loose (binds ADP+Pi), tight (synthesizes ATP). Each 360° rotation makes 3 ATP. Yoshida and Kinosita (1997) directly visualized rotation by attaching a fluorescent actin filament. Walker (1994) solved the F1 crystal structure. ATP synthase is the smallest known biological motor (8-10 nm). AP Bio Unit 3 expects mechanistic understanding linking H+ flow → mechanical rotation → conformational change → ATP synthesis.

questions.prompt: Green pigment in thylakoids that absorbs red and blue light, reflecting green.
questions.explanation: Chlorophyll a (peaks at 430 and 662 nm — blue and red) is the primary photosynthetic pigment; chlorophyll b (453 and 642 nm) is an accessory. Both contain a porphyrin ring chelating Mg2+ with a phytol hydrocarbon tail anchoring it in thylakoid membranes. Reflected green is why plants look green. Carotenoids (β-carotene, lutein) extend absorption to 400-500 nm and quench dangerous singlet O2. Engelmann (1882) showed photosynthesis peaks at red and blue using Spirogyra + aerotactic bacteria — a Beer's-Law absorption assay before the term existed. AP Bio Unit 3 lab: paper chromatography separates leaf pigments by Rf value.

questions.prompt: Photosystem that splits water and releases O2 as a byproduct.
questions.explanation: Photosystem II (PSII, with reaction center pigment P680) absorbs light at 680 nm, excites an electron to pheophytin, then through plastoquinone (PQ) to the cytochrome b6f complex. To replace the lost electron, PSII oxidizes water at the oxygen-evolving complex (OEC, Mn4CaO5 cluster): 2H2O → 4H+ + 4e- + O2. This is why we breathe — atmospheric O2 comes from this reaction over 2.5 billion years of cyanobacterial + plant photosynthesis. Joliot and Kok (1969) deduced the 5-state S-cycle. AP Bio Unit 3 FRQs ask students to trace electrons from H2O → PSII → PQ → cyt b6f → PC → PSI → ferredoxin → NADP+ — the Z-scheme.

questions.prompt: Reaction releasing free energy with negative ΔG; spontaneous.
questions.explanation: Exergonic reactions release free energy (ΔG < 0) and are spontaneous (kinetic, not necessarily fast — combustion of glucose has ΔG = -2870 kJ/mol but won't proceed at room temp without ignition or enzymes). Endergonic reactions (ΔG > 0) require energy input. Cells COUPLE exergonic and endergonic reactions: ATP hydrolysis (-30.5 kJ/mol) drives glutamate + NH4+ → glutamine (+14 kJ/mol; net -16.5 kJ/mol via glutamine synthetase). AP Bio Unit 3 expects students to interpret reaction-coordinate diagrams with reactants, transition state, products, and Ea — a Science Practice 1 modeling task.

questions.prompt: C4 plant strategy of fixing CO2 first into oxaloacetate via PEP carboxylase.
questions.explanation: C4 plants (corn, sugarcane, sorghum) fix CO2 first via PEP carboxylase (no oxygenase activity) in mesophyll cells, making 4C oxaloacetate → malate, which shuttles into bundle-sheath cells (Kranz anatomy) where it releases CO2 to concentrate it ~10× around RuBisCO, minimizing photorespiration. Hatch and Slack discovered this in the 1960s. ~3% of plant species are C4 but account for ~25% of terrestrial photosynthesis. Advantage in hot, dry, bright conditions (higher water-use efficiency); disadvantage in cool wet conditions (extra ATP cost). AP Bio Unit 3 compares C3, C4, and CAM strategies — a Science Practice 6 argumentation FRQ.

questions.prompt: Pigment-protein complex array that funnels excitation energy to reaction centers.
questions.explanation: Antenna complexes (light-harvesting complexes — LHCI and LHCII) contain ~200-300 chlorophyll a, b, and carotenoid molecules per reaction center. Energy absorbed by any pigment migrates by Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) to the reaction center P680 (PSII) or P700 (PSI), where charge separation occurs. This funneling amplifies the effective cross-section ~250× — without antennas, leaves would need vastly more reaction centers. State transitions (LHCII migrating between PSII and PSI based on plastoquinone redox state) balance photon flux between the two photosystems. AP Bio Unit 3 ties antenna function to quantum efficiency and the action spectrum.

questions.prompt: Enzyme fixing CO2 to RuBP — the most abundant protein on Earth.
questions.explanation: RuBisCO (Ribulose-1,5-Bisphosphate Carboxylase/Oxygenase, EC 4.1.1.39) catalyzes CO2 + RuBP → 2 × 3-PGA (3-phosphoglycerate) in the Calvin cycle stroma. It is the most abundant protein on Earth (~50% of leaf soluble protein, ~0.7 kg per person on the planet). It is also slow (~3 reactions/sec — orders of magnitude below most enzymes) and promiscuous: it also catalyzes the oxygenase reaction (RuBP + O2 → 3-PGA + 2-phosphoglycolate), causing photorespiration that wastes energy. C4 and CAM plants evolved CO2-concentrating mechanisms to minimize this. Andrew Benson and Melvin Calvin (1961 Nobel) traced 14C through the cycle in Chlorella.

questions.prompt: 5-carbon CO2 acceptor regenerated each turn of the Calvin cycle.
questions.explanation: RuBP (ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate, 5C) is the CO2 acceptor in the Calvin cycle. Three turns of the cycle fix 3 CO2, producing 6 × 3-PGA → 6 × G3P (using 6 ATP and 6 NADPH); 5 G3P are rearranged through a complex 10-enzyme regeneration phase (using 3 more ATP) to regenerate 3 RuBP, and 1 G3P exits as net product. Total cost: 9 ATP + 6 NADPH per G3P; 18 ATP + 12 NADPH per glucose (2 G3P). AP Bio Unit 3 FRQs ask students to balance carbons through the three Calvin cycle phases: carbon fixation, reduction, regeneration.

questions.prompt: Photosystem reducing NADP+ to NADPH; absorbs at 700 nm.
questions.explanation: Photosystem I (PSI, reaction center P700) accepts electrons from plastocyanin, re-energizes them with 700 nm light, and passes them to ferredoxin and then ferredoxin-NADP+ reductase (FNR) to make NADPH (the reducing power for the Calvin cycle). Cyclic electron flow: when NADPH is high but ATP is needed, electrons cycle PSI → ferredoxin → cyt b6f → PC → PSI, pumping more H+ and making extra ATP without making NADPH. PSI has the longest electron transfer chain known (~25 nm). AP Bio Unit 3 expects students to predict ATP/NADPH ratios under different light conditions and to compare cyclic vs. noncyclic electron flow.

questions.prompt: 3-carbon product of carbon fixation that gets reduced to G3P.
questions.explanation: 3-PGA (3-phosphoglycerate, 3C) is the first stable product of CO2 fixation. The unstable 6C intermediate (carboxylated RuBP) splits immediately into 2 × 3-PGA. Each 3-PGA is then phosphorylated by ATP (→ 1,3-BPG) and reduced by NADPH (→ G3P) — the only reduction step in the Calvin cycle, consuming all of photosynthesis's NADPH. Calvin and Benson (1961 Nobel) identified 3-PGA as the first 14C-labeled compound using pulse-chase chromatography on illuminated Chlorella — the famous 'lollipop apparatus.' AP Bio Unit 3 expects students to trace radiolabeled carbon through the cycle.

questions.prompt: Desert plant strategy of opening stomata at night and storing CO2 as malate.
questions.explanation: CAM (Crassulacean Acid Metabolism) plants — cacti, succulents, pineapple, agave, orchids — open stomata at night when temperatures are cool and humidity high (minimizing water loss), fix CO2 via PEP carboxylase into oxaloacetate → malate stored in vacuoles. By day, stomata close to conserve water, and malate releases CO2 for RuBisCO and the Calvin cycle. So C4 separates CO2 fixation SPATIALLY (mesophyll vs. bundle sheath); CAM separates it TEMPORALLY (night vs. day). Discovered in Crassulaceae (jade plants). Extreme water-use efficiency (~3-5× C3, ~2× C4). AP Bio Unit 3 + 8 link CAM to xeric ecology and drought adaptation.

questions.prompt: Cytoplasmic 10-step pathway splitting glucose to 2 pyruvate, yielding net 2 ATP and 2 NADH.
questions.explanation: Glycolysis (Embden-Meyerhof-Parnas pathway) occurs in the cytoplasm of all cells (anaerobes and aerobes alike — most ancient metabolic pathway). It has 10 enzymatic steps: investment phase (2 ATP spent on glucose → F-1,6-BP) and payoff phase (4 ATP, 2 NADH made; net = +2 ATP, +2 NADH per glucose; pyruvate × 2 produced). Key regulated enzymes: hexokinase (inhibited by G-6-P), phosphofructokinase-1 (PFK-1, allosterically inhibited by ATP/citrate, activated by AMP/F-2,6-BP — the rate-limiting step), pyruvate kinase. AP Bio Unit 3 FRQs require students to balance carbons, ATP, and NADH across the pathway.

questions.prompt: 8-step mitochondrial matrix cycle oxidizing acetyl-CoA, producing 3 NADH, 1 FADH2, 1 GTP, 2 CO2 per turn.
questions.explanation: The citric acid cycle (Krebs cycle, TCA cycle) was elucidated by Hans Krebs (1937, Nobel 1953). Acetyl-CoA (2C) combines with oxaloacetate (4C) via citrate synthase to form citrate (6C). Over 8 enzyme steps, citrate is oxidized back to OAA, releasing 2 CO2, generating 3 NADH, 1 FADH2, and 1 GTP/ATP (substrate-level phosphorylation by succinyl-CoA synthetase). Per glucose (which gives 2 acetyl-CoA): 6 NADH, 2 FADH2, 2 ATP. Key regulated enzymes: citrate synthase, isocitrate dehydrogenase, α-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase. The cycle is amphibolic: intermediates also feed amino acid + heme biosynthesis. AP Bio Unit 3 demands carbon/electron bookkeeping mastery.

questions.prompt: Pathway in liver synthesizing glucose from lactate, glycerol, and amino acids during fasting.
questions.explanation: Gluconeogenesis (mostly liver, some kidney) synthesizes glucose from non-carb precursors during fasting: lactate (from muscle), glycerol (from triglyceride breakdown), and glucogenic amino acids (e.g., alanine, glutamine). It is NOT a simple reversal of glycolysis — three irreversible glycolysis steps are bypassed by four enzymes: pyruvate carboxylase + PEPCK (bypass pyruvate kinase), F-1,6-BPase (bypass PFK-1), G-6-Pase (bypass hexokinase). Glucagon promotes it; insulin suppresses it. Net cost: 6 ATP per glucose. AP Bio Unit 3 expects students to recognize gluconeogenesis as the counterpart to glycolysis maintaining blood glucose during fasting.

questions.prompt: Total set of biochemical reactions in a cell, broken into catabolism and anabolism.
questions.explanation: Metabolism comprises catabolism (breakdown — exergonic, generates ATP and NADH/FADH2: glycolysis, citric acid cycle, β-oxidation, glycogenolysis) and anabolism (biosynthesis — endergonic, consumes ATP and reducing power: protein, lipid, nucleotide, polysaccharide synthesis). Pathways often share intermediates (acetyl-CoA is a metabolic hub) and are regulated by allosteric and covalent control. Hans Krebs (1937 Nobel 1953) mapped the citric acid cycle. AP Bio Unit 3 FRQs ask students to predict effects of inhibiting one enzyme on flux through interconnected pathways — Science Practice 6 argumentation.

questions.prompt: ATP-yield variation between cells using shuttle systems with glycolytic NADH.
questions.explanation: Glycolysis produces cytosolic NADH, but the inner mitochondrial membrane is impermeable to NADH. Two shuttles transfer reducing equivalents: (1) malate-aspartate shuttle (liver, heart, kidney) — preserves NADH equivalency → ~2.5 ATP per NADH; (2) glycerol-3-phosphate shuttle (skeletal muscle, brain) — converts to FADH2 → ~1.5 ATP per NADH. This explains why ATP yield per glucose ranges 30-32 depending on tissue. Also explains why hard-working muscles can recover ATP fast (G3P shuttle is faster but yields less). AP Bio Unit 3 emphasizes this as a Science Practice 5 quantitative reasoning point — predicting ATP differences across cell types.

questions.prompt: Anaerobic process regenerating NAD+ by reducing pyruvate to lactate in muscle.
questions.explanation: Lactic acid fermentation (NAD+ regeneration) lets glycolysis continue when O2 is limited (intense exercise, anaerobic conditions). Lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) reduces pyruvate to lactate using NADH → NAD+. Bacteria like Lactobacillus use this to acidify milk → yogurt, cheese, kimchi. In muscle, lactate is exported to liver where the Cori cycle (Cori and Cori 1947 Nobel) converts it back to glucose via gluconeogenesis. Net glycolysis + fermentation: 2 ATP per glucose (vs. ~30 aerobic). Fermentation is 'wasteful' but FAST — enables anaerobic ATP supply in seconds. AP Bio Unit 3 contrasts fermentation with aerobic respiration's full oxidation.

questions.prompt: End product of glycolysis with 3 carbons and a ketone group.
questions.explanation: Pyruvate (3C, CH3-CO-COO-) is the final product of glycolysis. Under aerobic conditions, it enters mitochondria via the pyruvate carrier and is oxidatively decarboxylated by the pyruvate dehydrogenase complex (PDC, three enzymes E1+E2+E3, ~9 MDa — one of the largest enzyme complexes) to acetyl-CoA, releasing CO2 and reducing NAD+ → NADH. Under anaerobic conditions, pyruvate accepts electrons from NADH to regenerate NAD+ (fermentation): → lactate in animal muscle and lactic acid bacteria; → ethanol + CO2 in yeast (via pyruvate decarboxylase + alcohol dehydrogenase). AP Bio Unit 3 expects students to compare fermentation yields (2 ATP) with aerobic respiration (~30-32 ATP).

questions.prompt: Yeast fermentation product used in bread, beer, and wine; CO2 makes bread rise.
questions.explanation: Alcoholic fermentation in Saccharomyces cerevisiae: pyruvate → acetaldehyde (by pyruvate decarboxylase, releasing CO2) → ethanol (by alcohol dehydrogenase, oxidizing NADH → NAD+). The CO2 leavens bread; the ethanol intoxicates beer/wine drinkers. Louis Pasteur (1857) proved fermentation requires living yeast — disproving spontaneous generation hypotheses. Buchner (1907 Nobel) later isolated cell-free yeast extract that still fermented, founding biochemistry. AP Bio Unit 3 FRQs ask students to predict the response of yeast to oxygen (Pasteur effect: yeast shifts from fermentation to respiration when O2 is added, slowing glucose consumption ~10-20×).

questions.prompt: Initial step where oxygen-dependent organisms regenerate NAD+ via the ETC, not fermentation.
questions.explanation: Aerobic respiration uses O2 as the terminal electron acceptor at Complex IV (cytochrome c oxidase), allowing full oxidation of glucose to 6 CO2 + 6 H2O with maximal energy capture (~30-32 ATP). The huge yield comes from coupling NADH (10 per glucose) and FADH2 (2 per glucose) reoxidation to chemiosmotic ATP synthesis. Without O2, the ETC backs up — NAD+ depleted, glycolysis stalls — unless fermentation regenerates NAD+. Compare yields: anaerobic 2 ATP; aerobic ~30 ATP — a 15-fold gain. AP Bio Unit 3 expects students to explain why aerobic life dominates and to calculate ATP yields for partial pathways (e.g., glucose blocked at step 4).

questions.prompt: Metabolic concept where ATP hydrolysis drives an otherwise endergonic reaction.
questions.explanation: Energy coupling links exergonic processes to endergonic ones via shared intermediates. In glycolysis, ATP hydrolysis phosphorylates glucose to G-6-P (hexokinase, ΔG = -16.7 kJ/mol net); this drives glucose into the cell and traps it. ATP synthase couples the exergonic proton flow down its gradient (ΔG ≈ -200 kJ/mol over 3 H+) to endergonic ADP+Pi → ATP synthesis. Mechanically, ATP synthase rotates ~150 times/sec (Yoshida, Kinosita rotation experiments 1997 — visualized fluorescently). AP Bio Unit 3 emphasizes coupling as the master strategy for cellular work.

questions.prompt: General term for a chemical messenger that binds a receptor to alter cell behavior.
questions.explanation: A ligand is any molecule (hormone, neurotransmitter, growth factor, gas, peptide) that binds a receptor with high specificity. Insulin, epinephrine, EGF, acetylcholine, NO, testosterone, and IL-2 are all ligands. Affinity is measured as Kd (dissociation constant) — typical hormones bind in nM range. Lefkowitz and Kobilka (2012 Nobel) characterized many ligand-receptor pairs, especially GPCRs. AP Bio Unit 4 expects students to distinguish hydrophilic ligands (bind surface receptors — peptides, catecholamines) from hydrophobic (cross membrane to intracellular receptors — steroid hormones, thyroid hormone, vitamin D).

questions.prompt: Receptor type with 7 transmembrane α-helices coupled to heterotrimeric G proteins.
questions.explanation: GPCRs are the largest receptor family (~800 human genes, ~30% of FDA-approved drug targets — including beta blockers, antihistamines, opioids, neuroleptics). They have 7 α-helical transmembrane segments. Ligand binding causes conformational change that activates a Gαβγ heterotrimer: Gα exchanges GDP for GTP, dissociates, and activates downstream effectors (adenylyl cyclase → cAMP, or phospholipase C → IP3+DAG+Ca2+). Lefkowitz and Kobilka won the 2012 Nobel for structural and functional characterization (β2-adrenergic receptor crystal structures). AP Bio Unit 4 demands tracing from epinephrine → β-receptor → Gs → AC → cAMP → PKA → glycogen breakdown.

questions.prompt: Receptor that dimerizes and autophosphorylates on tyrosine residues, e.g., insulin receptor.
questions.explanation: RTKs (~58 human members, including insulin receptor, EGFR, FGFR, VEGFR, PDGFR) typically dimerize on ligand binding, trans-autophosphorylate on tyrosine residues, and recruit SH2-domain-containing adaptors (Grb2, PI3K, PLCγ) — activating Ras/MAPK, PI3K/Akt, and PLCγ pathways. Constitutively active RTKs (HER2 in breast cancer, EGFR in lung cancer, BCR-ABL in CML) drive cancer. Imatinib/Gleevec (2001 FDA) is a BCR-ABL inhibitor — a paradigm targeted therapy. AP Bio Unit 4 + 6 link RTK overactivation to cancer (proto-oncogene → oncogene) and require pathway diagrams from ligand → phosphorylation → transcription.

questions.prompt: Direct cell-cell signaling through gap junctions or plasmodesmata in plants.
questions.explanation: Direct contact signaling moves ions and small molecules (<1000 Da) through gap junctions (connexin hexamers — connexons — pair across membranes in animals) or plasmodesmata (membrane-lined channels through plant cell walls). Cardiac muscle cells synchronize contraction via gap-junction electrical coupling (connexin-43 dysfunction causes arrhythmias). Plant cells share cytoplasm via the symplastic pathway through plasmodesmata. Juxtacrine signaling involves membrane-bound ligands (Notch-Delta, ephrin-Eph) — a distinct mechanism critical for developmental patterning. AP Bio Unit 4 expects students to compare animal and plant direct-contact systems.

questions.prompt: Ion channel receptor opened by neurotransmitter binding — fast synaptic signaling.
questions.explanation: Ligand-gated ion channels (ionotropic receptors) combine receptor + channel: ligand binding opens the channel for ms timescales. Examples: nicotinic acetylcholine receptor (nAChR — Na+/K+, neuromuscular junction; targets of curare, succinylcholine), GABAA (Cl-, inhibitory; target of benzodiazepines, barbiturates, ethanol), NMDA/AMPA glutamate receptors (Ca2+/Na+ at excitatory synapses, basis of long-term potentiation). Patch clamp (Neher and Sakmann 1991 Nobel) records single-channel currents. AP Bio Unit 4 contrasts ionotropic (fast, ms) with metabotropic GPCR signaling (slow, sec-min) — crucial for understanding nervous system speed.

questions.prompt: Cascade of molecular changes converting an external signal to a cellular response.
questions.explanation: Signal transduction is the conversion of extracellular signals (ligand-receptor binding) into intracellular responses, typically via reversible protein phosphorylation (Krebs and Fischer 1992 Nobel for protein kinases) and second messengers. Cascades amplify the signal — one ligand → millions of activated effectors (e.g., 1 epinephrine → 10^4 cAMP → 10^6 PKA → 10^8 glucose released). Pathways include MAPK (growth), PI3K/Akt (survival), PLC/PKC (Ca2+/diacylglycerol), JAK/STAT (immune/cytokines). AP Bio Unit 4 FRQs ask students to identify amplification, integration, and termination steps in pathway diagrams.

questions.prompt: Small molecule like cAMP, IP3, or Ca2+ that relays signal from receptor to effectors.
questions.explanation: Second messengers are small intracellular signaling molecules generated rapidly after receptor activation. cAMP (Sutherland 1971 Nobel — discovered for epinephrine response, made by adenylyl cyclase from ATP) activates PKA. IP3 (released from PIP2 by phospholipase C) opens ER Ca2+ channels, raising cytosolic Ca2+ (basal ~100 nM → 1000 nM). DAG activates PKC. cGMP regulates retinal phototransduction and smooth muscle relaxation (NO target — Viagra inhibits cGMP-degrading PDE5). Ca2+ activates calmodulin and many target proteins. AP Bio Unit 4 emphasizes second messengers as amplifiers and integrators of signaling.

questions.prompt: Reversible covalent modification adding a phosphate group to Ser, Thr, or Tyr.
questions.explanation: Phosphorylation by kinases (using ATP) on serine, threonine, or tyrosine residues changes protein conformation/activity. ~30% of human proteins are phosphorylated at some point. Counteracted by phosphatases (PP1, PP2A, PP2B/calcineurin). Krebs and Fischer (1992 Nobel) discovered phosphorylase kinase regulating glycogen phosphorylase — the first reversible covalent regulation. Tyrosine kinase signaling is largely metazoan; bacteria use mostly His/Asp two-component systems. AP Bio Unit 4 expects students to identify kinase cascade steps and predict effects of kinase or phosphatase inhibitors (e.g., caffeine inhibits PDE → ↑cAMP).

questions.prompt: Three-tiered kinase cascade ending in ERK that drives proliferation responses.
questions.explanation: The MAPK cascade is the prototypical three-kinase relay: MAPKKK (e.g., Raf) phosphorylates MAPKK (MEK), which phosphorylates MAPK (ERK1/2). Activated ERK enters the nucleus and phosphorylates transcription factors (Elk1, Fos, Jun → AP-1) driving cell-cycle entry (cyclin D expression). Upstream activator: Ras (small G-protein, GTP-bound = active). Ras mutations (K-Ras G12D, G12V) lock it ON, driving ~30% of all human cancers (lung, colorectal, pancreatic). Vemurafenib targets BRAF V600E in melanoma (2011 FDA). AP Bio Unit 4 + 6 expect mastery of MAPK as the link from growth factor signaling → cancer when dysregulated.

questions.prompt: Mechanism by which signals self-correct or shut off through negative feedback.
questions.explanation: Negative feedback terminates signaling and prevents runaway activation. Mechanisms include: (1) phosphatases dephosphorylate kinase targets (PP1, PP2A); (2) GTPases hydrolyze GTP on G-proteins (terminating Gα signaling); (3) receptor desensitization (β-arrestin recruitment, GRK phosphorylation, internalization); (4) feedback inhibition of upstream kinases (ERK phosphorylates and inhibits Raf); (5) regulated degradation (e.g., IκB resynthesis after NF-κB activation). Homeostasis (blood glucose, body temp, hormone levels) depends on negative feedback. AP Bio Unit 4 + 8 FRQs require students to draw feedback loops with sensor, integrator, and effector — Science Practice 1 modeling.

questions.prompt: Phase of interphase where DNA is synthesized, doubling chromosome content.
questions.explanation: S phase (DNA Synthesis) of interphase replicates the entire genome — ~6 billion bp in humans in ~8 hours. ~50,000 origins of replication fire in a strict spatial-temporal program (euchromatin early, heterochromatin late). DNA polymerase δ (lagging strand) and ε (leading strand) extend from RNA primers (synthesized by Pol α/primase). Cells in S phase are uniquely sensitive to DNA damage — checkpoint kinases ATR and Chk1 enforce arrest if replication forks stall. Hartwell (1971 — yeast cdc mutants), Hunt, and Nurse (cyclins, Cdks; 2001 Nobel) discovered cell-cycle regulators by genetic and biochemical screens. AP Bio Unit 4 FRQs test G1 → S transition (restriction point) signals.

questions.prompt: Major checkpoint deciding whether to commit to S phase, sensitive to growth factors and DNA damage.
questions.explanation: The G1 (restriction) checkpoint integrates growth factor signals, nutrient status, and DNA damage. Once past it, the cell is committed to division. Key players: Rb (retinoblastoma protein) sequesters E2F transcription factors; CDK4/6-cyclin D phosphorylates Rb → E2F release → S-phase gene expression. p53 senses DNA damage, induces p21, halts CDK activity. Pat Hartwell isolated yeast cdc mutants (1971) identifying these regulators. The restriction point is the most common cell-cycle control point altered in cancer (Rb loss in retinoblastoma — Knudson's two-hit hypothesis, 1971). AP Bio Unit 4 + 6 FRQs require explaining how mutations bypass this checkpoint.

questions.prompt: Anaphase trigger that proteolytically cleaves cohesin, separating sisters.
questions.explanation: The APC/C (Anaphase-Promoting Complex / Cyclosome — a ubiquitin ligase) ubiquitinates securin, marking it for proteasomal degradation. Released separase then cleaves cohesin (the ring holding sister chromatids together since S phase), and spindle pulling forces drag sisters to opposite poles. APC/C also degrades cyclin B (inactivating CDK1), driving mitotic exit. Activation of APC/C is gated by the spindle assembly checkpoint (Mad2, BubR1) — only released when ALL kinetochores are bipolarly attached. Hartwell, Hunt, Nurse won the 2001 Nobel for cell-cycle control. AP Bio Unit 4 expects mechanistic understanding of anaphase trigger.

questions.prompt: Phase when chromosomes are most condensed and visible under microscope.
questions.explanation: Mitosis (M phase) divides one nucleus into two genetically identical nuclei. Walther Flemming (1882) first described mitosis in salamander cells. Stages: prophase (chromosome condensation, mitotic spindle assembly from centrosomes), prometaphase (nuclear envelope breakdown, microtubule-kinetochore attachment), metaphase (chromosome alignment), anaphase (sister separation by separase + cohesin cleavage, poleward MT shortening), telophase (chromosome decondensation, nuclear envelope reformation). Cytokinesis (cleavage furrow in animals via actomyosin ring; cell plate in plants via vesicle fusion) physically splits the cell. AP Bio Unit 4 demands students sketch chromosome behavior in each phase.

questions.prompt: Inhibitor of cyclin-dependent kinases coded by tumor suppressor genes; lost in many cancers.
questions.explanation: CDK inhibitors (CKIs — INK4 family p16, p15; CIP/KIP family p21, p27, p57) bind and inhibit cyclin-CDK complexes, halting cell-cycle progression. p21 is induced by p53 (the 'guardian of the genome'; Vogelstein, Levine 1979) when DNA damage is detected, arresting G1/S. p16INK4a inhibits CDK4/6-cyclin D, blocking RB phosphorylation — p16 loss is common in melanoma and pancreatic cancer. Most cancers escape these brakes (Hanahan and Weinberg 2000 Hallmarks of Cancer: sustained proliferation, evading growth suppressors). Palbociclib (CDK4/6 inhibitor) treats hormone-receptor-positive breast cancer (2015 FDA). AP Bio Unit 4 + 6 link cell-cycle deregulation to cancer pathophysiology.

questions.prompt: Programmed cell death involving caspase activation and cell fragmentation.
questions.explanation: Apoptosis (Greek: 'falling away') is regulated, non-inflammatory cell death. Hallmarks: chromatin condensation, DNA fragmentation (180-bp ladder by CAD), cell shrinkage, blebbing, apoptotic bodies cleared by macrophages. Executed by caspases (cysteine-aspartate proteases): initiators (caspase-8 extrinsic, caspase-9 intrinsic) activate executioners (caspase-3, -6, -7) that cleave hundreds of substrates. Pathways: extrinsic (death receptors FAS, TNFR + ligand → caspase-8) and intrinsic (mitochondrial — Bax/Bak permeabilize outer membrane, cytochrome c release → apoptosome → caspase-9). Discovered through C. elegans developmental cell death (Brenner, Horvitz, Sulston 2002 Nobel). AP Bio Unit 4 expects this as a tumor-suppressing mechanism.

questions.prompt: Mitotic phase when chromosomes line up at the metaphase plate.
questions.explanation: Metaphase: sister-chromatid pairs align at the metaphase plate (cell equator), each kinetochore attached to spindle microtubules from opposite poles (bipolar attachment). Mitosis sequence: prophase (chromosome condensation, NE breakdown begins) → prometaphase (NE breakdown, MT capture by kinetochores) → metaphase (alignment) → anaphase (sister separation) → telophase (NE reforms, decondensation) → cytokinesis (cleavage furrow). Spindle assembly checkpoint (SAC, Mad2/BubR1) blocks anaphase entry until all kinetochores are properly attached — error here causes aneuploidy and is a cancer driver (Boveri 1914 first hypothesized). AP Bio Unit 4 demands phase identification on micrographs.

questions.prompt: Mitotic checkpoint detecting unattached kinetochores, blocking anaphase entry.
questions.explanation: The Spindle Assembly Checkpoint (SAC) prevents anaphase until every kinetochore is bipolarly attached to microtubules. Unattached kinetochores generate a 'wait' signal — the MCC (Mad2, BubR1, Bub3, Cdc20 complex) — that inhibits the APC/C and prevents securin/cyclin B degradation. Even ONE unattached kinetochore arrests mitosis. Bypassing the SAC causes chromosome missegregation and aneuploidy — virtually universal in solid tumors (Boveri 1914 first hypothesized cancer-aneuploidy link). Drugs: nocodazole and taxanes (paclitaxel) lock the SAC by perturbing MT dynamics → mitotic arrest → apoptosis (anti-cancer mechanism). AP Bio Unit 4 expects mechanistic knowledge of error-detection in mitosis.

questions.prompt: Tumor suppressor protein called the 'guardian of the genome,' often mutated in cancer.
questions.explanation: P53 (TP53 gene) is mutated in >50% of human cancers — the most-mutated tumor suppressor. Discovered 1979 by Lane, Crawford, Levine. In response to DNA damage (ATM/ATR phosphorylation), oncogene activation, or hypoxia, p53 protein is stabilized (Mdm2 degradation halts) and transcribes target genes: p21 (cell-cycle arrest), GADD45 (DNA repair), PUMA + Bax (apoptosis). Germline TP53 mutations cause Li-Fraumeni syndrome (multiple cancers, often early-onset). HPV E6 protein degrades p53 — mechanism of HPV-driven cervical cancer. AP Bio Unit 4 + 6 FRQs ask students to predict cell behavior when p53 is lost: failure to arrest with damage → mutation accumulation → cancer.

questions.prompt: Bcl-2 family balance determining whether intrinsic apoptosis proceeds.
questions.explanation: Intrinsic (mitochondrial) apoptosis is governed by Bcl-2 family proteins: pro-apoptotic (Bax, Bak — effectors that oligomerize and permeabilize the outer mitochondrial membrane; BH3-only proteins like Bid, Bim, Puma — sensors) vs. anti-apoptotic (Bcl-2, Bcl-xL, Mcl-1 — sequester pro-apoptotic). When the balance tips pro-apoptotic, MOMP (mitochondrial outer membrane permeabilization) releases cytochrome c → apoptosome (Apaf-1 + procaspase-9) → caspase-9 → caspase-3 → apoptosis. Bcl-2 was discovered as a t(14;18) translocation in follicular lymphoma (Korsmeyer 1985), the first oncogene that promoted survival rather than proliferation. Venetoclax (Bcl-2 inhibitor) treats CLL. AP Bio Unit 4 expects students to predict apoptosis outcomes from Bax/Bcl-2 ratios.

questions.prompt: Local chemical signal acting on neighboring cells, like growth factors and cytokines.
questions.explanation: Paracrine signaling targets nearby cells (μm range) via diffusion. Examples: growth factors (FGF, EGF, PDGF) in wound healing; cytokines (IL-1, TNF-α) in immune responses; neurotransmitters in synaptic clefts (technically synaptic signaling — even more localized). The signaling molecule is destroyed or removed quickly by extracellular enzymes (e.g., acetylcholinesterase breaks down ACh in <1 ms) or uptake (serotonin reuptake by SERT — target of SSRIs). Contrast with endocrine (long-range hormone, seconds to hours) and autocrine (cell signals itself — common in cancer for sustained growth). AP Bio Unit 4 FRQs require students to classify signaling by distance and time scale.

questions.prompt: Hormone-based long-distance signaling traveling through the bloodstream.
questions.explanation: Endocrine signaling: ductless glands (pituitary, thyroid, adrenals, pancreas, gonads) secrete hormones into the bloodstream that act on distant target cells expressing the appropriate receptor. Bayliss and Starling (1902) coined 'hormone' for secretin. Insulin (β-cells, glucose homeostasis), thyroxine (thyroid, metabolism), cortisol (adrenal cortex, stress), epinephrine (adrenal medulla, fight-or-flight), estrogen/testosterone (gonads, reproduction). Half-lives: peptides minutes; steroids hours. Hypothalamus-pituitary-target axes use multi-level negative feedback (TRH → TSH → T4 → feedback inhibition). AP Bio Unit 4 + 8 link hormone systems to homeostasis.

questions.prompt: Cell signaling where a cell secretes a molecule and responds to its own signal.
questions.explanation: Autocrine signaling: a cell produces a signal that acts on its own receptors. Common in immune cells (T cells secrete IL-2 to drive their own proliferation after activation) and in cancer (tumor cells often acquire autocrine growth factor loops — e.g., glioblastomas secreting and responding to PDGF, sustaining proliferation independent of external signals). Hanahan and Weinberg (2000) listed autocrine signaling as a hallmark of cancer. AP Bio Unit 4 + 6 expect students to predict consequences when normal paracrine signaling becomes autocrine via mutation.

questions.prompt: Intracellular receptor binding lipid-soluble hormones, acting as transcription factor.
questions.explanation: Steroid/nuclear receptors are transcription factors that bind lipid-soluble hormones (cortisol, aldosterone, testosterone, estrogen, progesterone, thyroid hormone, vitamin D, retinoic acid). Apo-receptors sit in cytoplasm or nucleus bound to chaperones (HSP90); ligand binding causes conformational change, dimerization, and DNA binding at hormone response elements (HREs) — altering gene expression over hours. Tamoxifen (estrogen receptor antagonist) treats breast cancer; mifepristone (progesterone receptor antagonist) is an abortifacient. Jensen (1958) discovered the estrogen receptor; Evans, Chambon, O'Malley pioneered nuclear receptor biology (2004 Lasker Prize). AP Bio Unit 4 + 6 link these to gene regulation.

questions.prompt: Cellular response coordinator in plants released as gas, triggering fruit ripening.
questions.explanation: Ethylene (C2H4) is a gaseous plant hormone that triggers fruit ripening (climacteric fruits: bananas, apples, tomatoes), leaf abscission, and stress responses (the 'triple response': stem thickening, shortening, horizontal growth). Synthesized from methionine via ACC (1-aminocyclopropane-1-carboxylate). Receptors (ETR1, ETR2) are membrane-bound histidine kinases — relatives of bacterial two-component systems, evolutionary relics from endosymbiotic chloroplasts. 1-MCP (1-methylcyclopropene) blocks ethylene receptors commercially to extend fruit shelf life. Burg and Burg (1960s) established ethylene's ripening role. AP Bio Unit 4 + 8 use ethylene as a plant hormone case study.

questions.prompt: Cell division producing four genetically distinct haploid gametes from one diploid cell.
questions.explanation: Meiosis reduces chromosome number from diploid (2n) to haploid (n) in two rounds of division (meiosis I and II) following ONE round of DNA replication. Meiosis I separates homologs (reduction division); meiosis II separates sisters (equational, like mitosis). Net result: 4 genetically unique haploid gametes per parent cell. Key sources of variation: independent assortment of homologs (2^23 = ~8 million combinations in humans), crossing over (chiasmata in prophase I), and random fertilization (~8 million × 8 million = 64 trillion zygote combinations from any couple). Sutton and Boveri (~1903) linked chromosome behavior in meiosis to Mendelian inheritance. AP Bio Unit 5 demands phase identification.

questions.prompt: Random orientation of homolog pairs at metaphase I, generating allele combinations.
questions.explanation: Mendel's second law (1866): alleles of different genes segregate independently into gametes — provided they are on different chromosomes (or far apart on the same chromosome). Mechanically, this arises in metaphase I: each homolog pair orients randomly (maternal/paternal can face either pole), with 2^n combinations for n chromosome pairs (humans: 2^23 ≈ 8.4 million combos before crossing over). Apparent exceptions (linked genes) require crossing over to recombine. Sutton-Boveri chromosomal theory (1902-03) gave Mendel's laws a physical basis. AP Bio Unit 5 FRQs commonly use Punnett squares and dihybrid 9:3:3:1 ratios to test mastery.

questions.prompt: Mitosis II-like phase separating sister chromatids in haploid cells.
questions.explanation: Meiosis II resembles mitosis in mechanics but begins with haploid (n) cells (each chromosome still has 2 sister chromatids from S phase). Prophase II → metaphase II (chromosomes align) → anaphase II (sisters separate at centromere) → telophase II (4 haploid cells). NO DNA replication between meiosis I and II. Errors in meiosis II nondisjunction produce gametes with 2 sisters (will be n+1 in zygote) or 0 (n-1). Most trisomies (Down syndrome trisomy 21, Edwards trisomy 18, Patau trisomy 13) arise from maternal meiosis I nondisjunction (>90%); meiosis II errors are less common. AP Bio Unit 5 demands students contrast meiosis I (homolog separation) vs. meiosis II (sister separation).

questions.prompt: Disorder caused by expansion of CAG repeats in a gene, with anticipation across generations.
questions.explanation: Trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders include Huntington's disease (HTT gene, CAG repeats; >36 = pathogenic, ~40-50 typical onset 40s, >60 juvenile onset; encodes mutant polyQ huntingtin causing striatal neuron death), Fragile X (FMR1, CGG, >200), myotonic dystrophy (DMPK, CTG), Friedreich's ataxia (FXN, GAA intronic). Anticipation: repeat number expands across generations, causing earlier and more severe onset in children — through paternal (HD) or maternal (myotonic, fragile X) transmission. HTT gene cloned 1993 (Gusella). AP Bio Unit 5 + 6 FRQs use HD as autosomal dominant + late-onset + ethical-counseling cases.

questions.prompt: Failure of chromosomes to separate properly during meiosis, causing aneuploidy.
questions.explanation: Nondisjunction is failure of homologs (meiosis I) or sister chromatids (meiosis II or mitosis) to separate, yielding gametes with extra or missing chromosomes (aneuploidy). Down syndrome (trisomy 21, 1:700 births), Turner (X0, 1:2500 girls), Klinefelter (XXY, 1:500 boys), Edwards (trisomy 18), Patau (trisomy 13) are major examples. Maternal age strongly increases meiosis I nondisjunction (oocytes arrested in prophase I from fetal development until ovulation — decades of accumulated damage). Most autosomal aneuploidies are embryonic lethal except 13, 18, 21 (small chromosomes, few genes). AP Bio Unit 5 FRQs commonly use pedigrees with affected offspring and require nondisjunction identification.

questions.prompt: Allele expressed in the heterozygote, masking the recessive partner.
questions.explanation: A dominant allele (uppercase, e.g., A) is expressed in heterozygous (Aa) and homozygous (AA) genotypes, masking the recessive allele. Mendel (1866, Brünn paper rediscovered 1900) established this with pea-plant 3:1 ratios in F2 monohybrid crosses (round R vs. wrinkled r; yellow Y vs. green y). Note: 'dominant' refers to phenotype expression in heterozygotes, NOT frequency in a population (a dominant allele can be rare; Huntington's disease HD allele is dominant but rare). Many human conditions are autosomal dominant: Huntington's (HD/CAG repeat), achondroplasia (FGFR3), Marfan (FBN1). AP Bio Unit 5 FRQs use pedigrees to identify mode of inheritance.

questions.prompt: Probability rule for either of two mutually exclusive events occurring.
questions.explanation: The sum rule: P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A and B); for mutually exclusive events, P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B). In genetics: probability of homozygous (AA or aa) offspring from Aa × Aa = P(AA) + P(aa) = 1/4 + 1/4 = 1/2. Distinct from product (multiplication) rule used for independent events. AP Bio Unit 5 FRQs commonly ask multi-gene probability calculations: P(AaBbCc child from AaBb × AaBb × AaBb) requires product rule for each gene; P(at least one Bb child in three children) uses sum rule. Mastery saves time over drawing 64-cell Punnett squares.

questions.prompt: Probability tool predicting offspring genotypes from parental gametes.
questions.explanation: Reginald Punnett (1905, Cambridge) devised the Punnett square as a probabilistic tool for predicting offspring genotype/phenotype ratios from parental gametes. Monohybrid Aa × Aa → 1 AA: 2 Aa: 1 aa (3:1 phenotype). Dihybrid AaBb × AaBb → 9:3:3:1 phenotype ratio (assuming independent assortment). Sex-linked crosses include X^A X^a × X^A Y or similar.
