Beyond 3 genes, Punnett squares become unwieldy (8 × 8 = 64 cells for trihybrid) — use multiplication rule instead (P(AaBbCc from AaBb x AaBb x AaBb) = (1/2)·(1/2)·(1/2) = 1/8). AP Bio Unit 5 demands both Punnett-square and probability-rule competence.

questions.prompt: Probability rule for two independent events both occurring.
questions.explanation: The product rule: P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B) for independent events. In genetics: P(AaBb from AaBb × AaBb) = P(Aa) × P(Bb) = 1/2 × 1/2 = 1/4 (assuming independent assortment). Critical for multigene problems and pedigrees. P(carrier × carrier → all three children affected) = 1/4 × 1/4 × 1/4 = 1/64. Birth order matters → use binomial distribution: P(2 affected + 1 unaffected in 3 children, any order) = C(3,2) × (1/4)² × (3/4) = 3 × 1/16 × 3/4 = 9/64. AP Bio Unit 5 FRQs reward students who can fluently combine sum and product rules with binomial expansions.

questions.prompt: ABO blood group genetics: A and B are both expressed in AB heterozygote.
questions.explanation: Codominance: both alleles fully expressed in the heterozygote, producing distinct phenotypes (NOT blended like incomplete dominance). Classic example: ABO blood group (Landsteiner 1900, Nobel 1930). IA codes A antigen (GalNAc terminal sugar); IB codes B antigen (Gal); i is null. Genotypes: IAIA or IAi = A; IBIB or IBi = B; IAIB = AB (both A and B antigens, codominant); ii = O (no terminal sugar). AB is the universal acceptor (no anti-A or anti-B antibodies); O is the universal donor (no antigens to trigger reaction). Roan coat color in cattle (R red allele + r white = roan with intermixed red and white hairs) is another codominance example. AP Bio Unit 5 FRQs commonly use ABO + Rh blood group inheritance problems.

questions.prompt: Genes carried on sex chromosomes, displaying inheritance patterns differing by sex.
questions.explanation: Sex-linked genes are on X (most) or Y chromosomes. X-linked recessive (red-green color blindness, hemophilia A/B, Duchenne muscular dystrophy) shows characteristic patterns: males (XY) need only one allele to express; females (XX) need two. Affected fathers + carrier mothers → 50% affected sons, 50% carrier daughters. Trait skips generations through carrier females. Morgan's white-eyed Drosophila (1910) was the first sex-linked mutation discovered, establishing the chromosomal theory of inheritance (Nobel 1933). Lyon hypothesis (1961): one X is randomly inactivated in female mammalian cells (Barr body) — mosaic expression visible as calico cat coat patches. AP Bio Unit 5 FRQs require pedigree analysis to identify X-linked recessive vs. autosomal.

questions.prompt: Statistical test comparing observed vs. expected offspring ratios.
questions.explanation: Chi-square (χ²) = Σ[(observed - expected)² / expected] tests whether observed counts deviate significantly from a hypothesis. AP Bio uses χ² for Mendelian ratio testing (e.g., 9:3:3:1, 3:1, 1:1) and Hardy-Weinberg (Unit 7). Degrees of freedom = number of categories - 1; critical value at α = 0.05 from a chi-square table. If χ² < critical value → fail to reject null hypothesis (observed matches expected); χ² > critical → reject (significant deviation, e.g., evidence of linkage in a dihybrid cross). Karl Pearson (1900) developed χ² statistics. AP Bio Unit 5 + 7 FRQs require students to set up the calculation, identify df, and interpret with the critical value table provided on the exam.

questions.prompt: Two or more genes contributing additively to a phenotype, often giving continuous variation.
questions.explanation: Polygenic inheritance: multiple genes contribute additively to a quantitative trait. Examples: human height (~700 SNPs explain ~25% of heritability — Visscher GWAS studies); skin color (multiple MC1R, SLC24A5, TYR, OCA2 loci); intelligence (IQ ~50% heritable, hundreds of small-effect loci). Produces a normal (bell-shaped) distribution in populations rather than discrete classes. Galton (1880s) first studied human polygenic traits statistically; the Hardy-Weinberg framework (Unit 7) and modern QTL mapping/GWAS extend this. AP Bio Unit 5 expects students to distinguish discrete Mendelian traits from continuous polygenic ones — Science Practice 5 graph reading.

questions.prompt: One gene's allele masking expression of a different gene at another locus.
questions.explanation: Epistasis: one gene at one locus masks expression of another gene at a different locus. Yields modified dihybrid ratios (9:3:4, 12:3:1, 9:7, 13:3, 15:1 — all from a 9:3:3:1 base). Labrador retriever coat color: B (black/brown pigment) + E (deposition); ee genotype masks B locus → yellow lab regardless of B status (ratio 9 B_E_: 3 bbE_: 4 (B_ee + bbee) = black: brown: yellow). Bombay phenotype: hh masks ABO (no H precursor → no A or B antigens despite IA/IB alleles). AP Bio Unit 5 FRQs ask students to predict deviations from 9:3:3:1 and identify the epistatic relationship from offspring counts.

questions.prompt: Diagnostic technique amplifying specific DNA segments using primers and Taq polymerase.
questions.explanation: PCR (Kary Mullis 1983, Nobel 1993) exponentially amplifies a target DNA segment via thermocycling: denaturation 95°C (strand separation), annealing ~55°C (primers bind), extension 72°C (Taq polymerase extends). 30 cycles → 2^30 = ~10^9-fold amplification. Taq polymerase from Thermus aquaticus (Brock 1969 Yellowstone hot springs) is thermostable, allowing automation. Variations: RT-PCR (cDNA from mRNA), qPCR (real-time fluorescence quantification — basis of COVID-19 testing), digital PCR. AP Bio Unit 5 + 6 FRQs ask students to design PCR primers, calculate amplification, and interpret gel band patterns — Science Practice 4 (experimental design).

questions.prompt: Genes on the same chromosome that tend to be inherited together.
questions.explanation: Linked genes are located close together on the same chromosome and tend to be inherited together (deviate from 9:3:3:1 dihybrid ratio). Morgan (1911) discovered linkage in Drosophila (white eyes + yellow body genes both X-linked). The closer two genes, the more linked; far-apart genes on the same chromosome assort almost independently because of frequent crossing over between them. AP Bio Unit 5 expects students to recognize linkage from offspring counts that deviate from expected ratios in dihybrid crosses and to calculate recombination frequency (RF) = recombinant offspring / total offspring.

questions.prompt: Prophase I event where homologous chromosomes pair and exchange segments.
questions.explanation: Crossing over occurs in pachytene of prophase I after homologous chromosomes synapse (pair lengthwise) via the synaptonemal complex. Spo11 introduces programmed double-strand breaks; subsequent resection and strand invasion lead to chiasmata (visible cross-over points) and reciprocal exchange of segments between homologs. ~50-60 crossovers per human meiosis (~1-3 per chromosome arm). This generates genetic variation among gametes by recombining maternal and paternal alleles. Morgan (1911) used crossover frequency to construct the first genetic linkage maps in Drosophila (1 cM = 1% recombination). AP Bio Unit 5 expects mechanistic mastery of crossover and its evolutionary consequences.

questions.prompt: Test cross used to determine whether a dominant individual is homozygous or heterozygous.
questions.explanation: A testcross mates an individual showing the dominant phenotype with a homozygous recessive (aa). If offspring all show dominant phenotype, parent was AA; if 1:1 dominant:recessive split, parent was Aa. Mendel used testcrosses to confirm his hypothesis of paired hereditary factors before he had a name for genes. AP Bio Unit 5 FRQs often present testcross data and ask students to determine genotype with statistical reasoning (chi-square goodness-of-fit at α = 0.05 with appropriate degrees of freedom — Science Practice 5 quantitative skill). Three-point testcrosses also map gene linkage by recombination frequency.

questions.prompt: Percent recombinant gametes used as a measure of distance between linked genes.
questions.explanation: Recombination frequency (RF) = (recombinant offspring / total offspring) × 100% = map distance in centiMorgans (cM); 1 cM ≈ 1% recombination ≈ 1 Mb of DNA on average in humans (varies by region). Maximum RF is 50% (= unlinked, indistinguishable from independent assortment). Sturtevant (1913, Morgan lab undergraduate) built the first genetic map using crossover data from Drosophila. Modern mapping uses SNP genotyping, RNA-seq, and Hi-C. AP Bio Unit 5 FRQs give recombinant offspring counts and ask students to compute map distance — pure Science Practice 5 quantitative reasoning.

questions.prompt: Genome-wide approach mapping disease loci by tracking SNP associations.
questions.explanation: GWAS scans hundreds of thousands to millions of SNPs across thousands of cases vs. controls, identifying loci with genotypic differences (Manhattan plots show -log10 P-values across the genome; peaks crossing the Bonferroni threshold (5×10^-8) are putative associations). First major GWAS (2005, Klein et al., age-related macular degeneration, CFH gene). Has identified thousands of loci for type 2 diabetes (TCF7L2 strongest), height (hundreds), schizophrenia, Crohn's, IBD. GWAS finds COMMON variants (MAF >5%) with small effect sizes — rare large-effect variants need different methods (sequencing-based). AP Bio Unit 5 + 6 use GWAS as exemplar quantitative genetics.

questions.prompt: Single gene affecting multiple unrelated phenotypic traits, like sickle-cell.
questions.explanation: Pleiotropy: one gene affects multiple seemingly unrelated phenotypes. Sickle-cell anemia (HBB Glu6Val): anemia, painful crises, organ infarcts, splenomegaly, jaundice — AND heterozygote advantage in malaria-endemic regions. Marfan syndrome (FBN1 fibrillin-1): tall stature, long limbs, lens dislocation, aortic aneurysm — Abraham Lincoln may have had it. Cystic fibrosis (CFTR ΔF508): thick lung mucus, pancreatic insufficiency, infertility (men), elevated sweat chloride. PKU (phenylalanine hydroxylase deficiency): intellectual disability, fair skin, eczema (multiple downstream effects of phe accumulation). AP Bio Unit 5 + 6 expect students to explain pleiotropy mechanistically — one protein in multiple pathways.

questions.prompt: Heterozygote phenotype intermediate between homozygotes (e.g., pink from red × white).
questions.explanation: Incomplete dominance: heterozygote (Aa) has an intermediate phenotype between the two homozygotes. Snapdragons (Antirrhinum): red RR × white rr → pink Rr (insufficient pigment from single R allele to make full red). Familial hypercholesterolemia: LDLR heterozygotes have 2× normal cholesterol; homozygotes have 4-6× and develop coronary disease in childhood (Brown and Goldstein 1985 Nobel). F2 ratio: 1:2:1 phenotype = genotype. Distinct from codominance (both alleles expressed independently, e.g., ABO blood group AB phenotype). Distinct from complete dominance (heterozygote = homozygote dominant phenotype). AP Bio Unit 5 FRQs use these as graph-reading and ratio-prediction stems.

questions.prompt: Diagram showing family inheritance across generations, with affected and unaffected.
questions.explanation: Pedigrees use standard symbols: circles (♀), squares (♂), filled (affected), half-filled (carrier), Roman numerals for generations, Arabic for individuals. Analysis steps: (1) determine if trait is dominant or recessive (skipped generations → recessive); (2) determine autosomal vs. sex-linked (affected fathers passing to all daughters → X-linked dominant; mother-to-son with skipping → X-linked recessive); (3) calculate carrier probabilities using conditional probability and Bayes' theorem. AP Bio Unit 5 FRQs give a pedigree, ask mode of inheritance, then ask the probability the next child will be affected — a multi-step Science Practice 6 argumentation question.

questions.prompt: Inheritance pattern from mitochondrial DNA: passed exclusively from mother.
questions.explanation: Mitochondrial DNA (mtDNA, ~16.5 kb circular, 37 genes encoding 13 OXPHOS subunits, 2 rRNAs, 22 tRNAs) is inherited maternally — sperm mitochondria are typically ubiquitinated and degraded after fertilization. Wallace and colleagues (1988) used this to identify the 'mitochondrial Eve' ~150-200 kya in Africa. Mitochondrial diseases (Leber's hereditary optic neuropathy/LHON, MELAS, MERRF, Leigh syndrome) inherit only through females, affect all offspring of affected mothers, and show heteroplasmy (mix of mutant + WT mtDNA in each cell with threshold effects). Chloroplast DNA similarly shows maternal/uniparental inheritance in most plants. AP Bio Unit 5 + 7 use mtDNA for tracing evolutionary lineages and identifying nondisjunction-distinct inheritance.

questions.prompt: Calculator method for combining multiple uncertain inheritance probabilities.
questions.explanation: Bayesian analysis combines prior probabilities with new evidence to update genetic risk estimates. Example: a 30-year-old woman has 50% prior chance she's a Huntington's carrier (one affected parent). If she remains asymptomatic at 30, the likelihood she's a carrier decreases (because most carriers begin showing symptoms by 40-50). Bayes: P(carrier|asymptomatic at 30) = [P(asymptomatic at 30 | carrier) × P(carrier)] / [P(asymptomatic at 30 | carrier) × P(carrier) + P(asymptomatic at 30 | non-carrier) × P(non-carrier)]. AP Bio Unit 5 uses simpler conditional reasoning in pedigree problems: given parental genotypes, what is probability of next child being affected — Science Practice 5 quantitative skill.

questions.prompt: Enzyme synthesizing DNA in 5'→3' direction using a template.
questions.explanation: DNA polymerases synthesize new DNA in 5'→3' direction by adding dNTPs to the 3'-OH of a primer. In E. coli, Pol III is the main replicative enzyme (1000 nt/s, processivity ~5×10^5 nt via β-clamp loader); Pol I removes RNA primers and fills the gap (Kornberg 1956 Nobel for discovering DNA Pol I). In eukaryotes, Pol α/primase makes RNA primer + initial DNA; Pol δ handles lagging strand; Pol ε handles leading strand. Proofreading 3'→5' exonuclease (Pol I, Pol III ε subunit, Pol δ/ε) keeps error rate ~10^-7; with mismatch repair, ~10^-10 per bp per generation. AP Bio Unit 6 demands enzyme-by-enzyme replication fork mastery.

questions.prompt: Enzyme that unwinds the DNA double helix at the replication fork.
questions.explanation: Helicases (ATP-dependent motor proteins) unwind double-stranded DNA at replication forks. In E. coli, DnaB (homohexameric ring) translocates 5'→3' on the lagging strand, separating strands ahead. In eukaryotes, the CMG complex (Cdc45-MCM2-7-GINS) is the replicative helicase. Single-strand binding proteins (SSB in prokaryotes, RPA in eukaryotes) bind exposed ssDNA to prevent reannealing/damage. Topoisomerases (DNA gyrase in bacteria, Topo I/II in eukaryotes) relieve supercoiling ahead of the fork — quinolone antibiotics (ciprofloxacin) inhibit bacterial gyrase. AP Bio Unit 6 FRQs require students to map all replication fork enzymes onto a labeled diagram — Science Practice 1 modeling.

questions.prompt: Enzyme placing RNA primers needed for DNA polymerase to begin synthesis.
questions.explanation: Primase synthesizes short (~10 nt) RNA primers providing the 3'-OH needed for DNA polymerases. In E. coli, DnaG primase is recruited by helicase (DnaB) and makes ~one primer per ~1000 nt on the lagging strand. In eukaryotes, the four-subunit DNA polymerase α/primase complex makes RNA primer + extends with DNA (~30 nt) before handing off to Pol δ/ε. Primers are later removed by RNase H (eukaryotes) or Pol I 5'→3' exonuclease (prokaryotes) and replaced with DNA. AP Bio Unit 6 FRQs commonly ask why a primer is needed (DNA polymerase cannot initiate synthesis de novo) and why primers are RNA (allows error-prone start to be excised + repaired).

questions.prompt: Enzyme sealing nicks in DNA backbone after primer removal/Okazaki fragment processing.
questions.explanation: DNA ligase seals the phosphodiester backbone between Okazaki fragments (~100-200 nt in eukaryotes, 1000-2000 nt in prokaryotes) on the lagging strand, using ATP or NAD+ as a cofactor. Discovered 1967 by labs of Lehman, Gellert, Hurwitz, and Richardson independently. T4 DNA ligase from bacteriophage is the workhorse of molecular cloning, joining sticky and blunt ends in recombinant DNA work. Defects in human DNA ligase IV cause LIG4 syndrome (immunodeficiency, radiosensitivity). AP Biology Unit 6 tests replication fork architecture (DNA pol III, primase, helicase, SSB) — ligase is the final step that gives the lagging strand continuity.

questions.prompt: Enzyme adding repetitive sequences to chromosome ends to prevent shortening.
questions.explanation: Telomerase is a ribonucleoprotein reverse transcriptase (TERT protein + TERC RNA template) that extends 3' ends of telomeres by adding TTAGGG repeats (humans). Greider and Blackburn (1985, Tetrahymena; Nobel 2009 with Szostak) discovered it solving the 'end-replication problem' identified by Olovnikov and Watson independently in the early 1970s. Most somatic cells lack telomerase, so chromosomes shorten ~50-100 bp/division → cellular senescence after ~50-80 divisions (Hayflick limit, 1961). Stem cells, germ cells, and ~90% of cancers express telomerase (immortalization). AP Bio Unit 6 + 7 link telomerase to aging, cancer biology, and replicative immortality — Hanahan-Weinberg Hallmarks of Cancer.

questions.prompt: Enzyme reading DNA template to synthesize RNA in 5'→3' direction.
questions.explanation: RNA polymerase synthesizes RNA from a DNA template in 5'→3' direction, NO primer needed (unlike DNA polymerase). Eukaryotes have three nuclear RNA pols: Pol I makes rRNA in nucleolus (~80% of cell's RNA); Pol II makes all mRNA + many small RNAs (target of α-amanitin from Amanita phalloides death cap mushroom); Pol III makes tRNA and 5S rRNA. Bacteria have one core RNA Pol (αα ββ' ω) + σ factor for promoter recognition. Roger Kornberg (2006 Nobel) solved Pol II crystal structure. AP Bio Unit 6 FRQs require comparing prokaryotic vs. eukaryotic transcription mechanics.

questions.prompt: 5' end modification adding 7-methylguanosine to nascent eukaryotic mRNA.
questions.explanation: The 5' cap is added co-transcriptionally to nascent mRNA: a 7-methylguanosine connected via an unusual 5'-5' triphosphate linkage to the first nucleotide. Functions: (1) ribosome recruitment via eIF4E for translation initiation; (2) protection from 5'→3' exonucleases (XRN1) — most mRNAs decay via decapping; (3) splicing efficiency; (4) nuclear export. mRNAs without caps are rapidly degraded. Cordycepin and ribavirin disrupt capping. AP Bio Unit 6 expects students to know the three mRNA processing steps: 5' cap, polyadenylation (3' poly-A tail), and intron splicing — all happening co-transcriptionally on Pol II's CTD platform.

questions.prompt: Operon protein blocking transcription when bound to DNA; e.g., LacI.
questions.explanation: Repressors are DNA-binding proteins that block RNA polymerase access to a promoter. In the lac operon, LacI repressor binds the operator and prevents transcription; allolactose (or IPTG in lab) binds LacI, inducing conformational change that releases it from DNA — allowing expression when lactose is present (induction). In the trp operon, TrpR repressor is INACTIVE alone; tryptophan binding ACTIVATES it (corepressor) → represses transcription when Trp is abundant (negative feedback). Different logic: lac = catabolic, ON when substrate present; trp = anabolic, OFF when product abundant. AP Bio Unit 6 FRQs require students to predict expression under various molecular conditions for both operon types.

questions.prompt: RNA segment removed during processing; non-coding portion of pre-mRNA.
questions.explanation: Introns (intervening sequences) are non-coding RNA segments within pre-mRNA, removed by the spliceosome (snRNPs U1, U2, U4, U5, U6 + ~150 proteins, one of the largest molecular machines, ~12 MDa). The exons (coding portions) are joined together to form mature mRNA. Introns were discovered 1977 by Sharp and Roberts (Nobel 1993) in adenovirus mRNA. Most human genes have multiple introns (mean ~9 per gene; >150 kb introns in DMD). Alternative splicing generates protein diversity: >95% of multi-exon human genes show alternative splicing, producing ~3-5 isoforms per gene on average. Disease example: β-thalassemia from intron splicing mutations in HBB. AP Bio Unit 6 + 5 link splicing to phenotypic variation.

questions.prompt: Process where one gene produces multiple mature mRNAs through differential intron/exon inclusion.
questions.explanation: Alternative splicing produces multiple mRNA isoforms from one pre-mRNA by including/excluding exons or using alternative splice sites. >95% of human multi-exon genes alternative-splice; mean ~3-5 isoforms per gene. Mechanisms include exon skipping, mutually exclusive exons, alternative 5'/3' splice sites, and intron retention. Drosophila Dscam (Down syndrome cell adhesion molecule) can generate 38,016 isoforms from one gene — huge for neuronal diversity. SR proteins promote exon inclusion; hnRNPs often promote skipping. Disease: spinal muscular atrophy (SMN2 splicing defect — nusinersen 2016 FDA antisense oligonucleotide corrects it). AP Bio Unit 6 expects students to explain how alternative splicing yields proteome expansion beyond the ~20,000 human gene count.

questions.prompt: Three-base codon table mapping nucleotides to amino acids; nearly universal.
questions.explanation: The genetic code (Nirenberg, Khorana, Holley 1968 Nobel) is read in 3-base codons: 64 codons (4^3) encode 20 amino acids + start (AUG, also Met) + 3 stop codons (UAA, UAG, UGA). The code is redundant (most aa have 2-6 codons), unambiguous (each codon → 1 aa), and nearly universal (some mitochondrial exceptions: UGA = Trp in mitochondria). Wobble at the third position: tRNA anticodon can recognize multiple codons differing only in their 3rd base (Crick 1966 wobble hypothesis). Nirenberg synthesized poly-U RNA → made polyphenylalanine, deducing UUU = Phe (1961). AP Bio Unit 6 FRQs provide the codon chart and ask students to translate mRNA or predict effects of point mutations.

questions.prompt: Codon initiating translation; usually AUG codes for methionine.
questions.explanation: AUG is the start codon, encoding methionine (Met) and initiating translation. In bacteria, initiation uses fMet-tRNA^fMet (N-formylmethionine — distinctive bacterial signature); in eukaryotes, regular Met-tRNA^Met-i (initiator). The Shine-Dalgarno sequence in bacterial mRNAs (~5-10 nt upstream of AUG) and Kozak sequence in eukaryotic mRNAs (~ACCATGG context) help ribosomes find the correct start. Internal ribosome entry sites (IRES) allow cap-independent initiation in some viral and cellular mRNAs. AP Bio Unit 6 expects students to identify the start of a reading frame and translate a given mRNA sequence — frequent FRQ task.

questions.prompt: Three codons signaling end of translation, recognized by release factors.
questions.explanation: The three stop codons (UAA = ochre, UAG = amber, UGA = opal) terminate translation. They are recognized not by tRNAs but by release factors (RF1 recognizes UAA/UAG; RF2 recognizes UAA/UGA in E. coli; eRF1 in eukaryotes recognizes all three). RFs trigger peptidyl transferase to release the peptide chain from the P-site tRNA and the ribosome to dissociate. Nonsense mutations (sense codon → stop) cause premature termination and truncated proteins — Duchenne muscular dystrophy (~10% from DMD nonsense mutations), cystic fibrosis (G542X), familial hypercholesterolemia. Read-through drugs (ataluren) suppress some nonsense mutations. AP Bio Unit 6 FRQs ask students to predict effects of single-base substitutions creating stop codons.

questions.prompt: Adaptor RNA carrying an amino acid and reading mRNA codons.
questions.explanation: TRNAs (~76 nt, cloverleaf 2° structure, L-shaped 3°) are the adaptors of translation. Each tRNA has a specific anticodon (reads mRNA codon by base pairing) and 3'-CCA end where the cognate amino acid is attached by an aminoacyl-tRNA synthetase (one per amino acid; 20 synthetases — ensuring proper codon-aa matching, the 'second genetic code'). Holley sequenced the first tRNA (yeast alanine tRNA, 1965 Nobel 1968). Charged tRNA enters ribosomal A site, peptide bond forms with P-site peptidyl-tRNA, ribosome translocates one codon. ~30-50 different tRNA species per cell handle the 61 sense codons via wobble. AP Bio Unit 6 demands mechanistic mastery of tRNA function in the ribosomal cycle.

questions.prompt: Cellular machine of rRNA + proteins catalyzing peptide bond formation; ribozyme.
questions.explanation: Ribosomes (~2.5 MDa, 65-70% rRNA, 30-35% protein) translate mRNA into protein. Prokaryotic 70S (50S + 30S subunits, target of streptomycin, tetracycline, chloramphenicol, erythromycin — basis of selective antibiotics). Eukaryotic 80S (60S + 40S). The peptidyl transferase center (PTC) is in the 50S/60S large subunit and is composed of rRNA — making the ribosome a ribozyme (Cech-Altman 1989 Nobel, Steitz, Yonath, Ramakrishnan 2009 Nobel for ribosome structure). Three tRNA sites: A (aminoacyl, incoming), P (peptidyl, chain), E (exit). Translation rate: ~20 aa/s in bacteria, ~5 aa/s in eukaryotes. AP Bio Unit 6 demands ribosomal cycle understanding: initiation, elongation (decoding, peptidyl transfer, translocation), termination, recycling.

questions.prompt: Bacterial gene cluster transcribed together as one mRNA with shared regulation.
questions.explanation: Operons (Jacob and Monod 1961, Nobel 1965) are clusters of co-regulated genes transcribed as one polycistronic mRNA in bacteria. Classic examples: lac operon (lacZ, lacY, lacA — lactose metabolism, induced by lactose/IPTG via repressor inactivation), trp operon (tryptophan biosynthesis genes, repressed when Trp is abundant). Components: promoter (RNA pol binding), operator (repressor/activator binding), structural genes. cAMP-CAP (catabolite activator protein) provides positive regulation tying gene expression to glucose levels (glucose ↓ → cAMP ↑ → CAP-cAMP binds → activates lac transcription IF lactose is present). AP Bio Unit 6 expects students to predict lac operon expression under all four glucose ± lactose combinations.

questions.prompt: Protein machine that removes introns, made of snRNPs.
questions.explanation: The spliceosome is a dynamic ribonucleoprotein complex (~12 MDa, larger than ribosome) of five small nuclear ribonucleoproteins (snRNPs: U1, U2, U4, U5, U6) plus ~150 proteins. It catalyzes two transesterification reactions to remove introns: (1) 2'-OH of branch point A attacks 5' splice site → lariat intermediate; (2) 3'-OH of upstream exon attacks 3' splice site → joined exons + released lariat. The catalytic core is RNA-based (U2/U6) — making the spliceosome a ribozyme. Sharp and Roberts (Nobel 1993) discovered splicing; Cech and Altman (Nobel 1989) established ribozyme catalysis. AP Bio Unit 6 emphasizes splicing as a regulatory point — alternative splicing dramatically expands proteome diversity.

questions.prompt: DNA element binding transcription factors and activating distant genes.
questions.explanation: Enhancers are DNA elements (~50-1500 bp) that bind activator TFs and stimulate transcription of distant genes (sometimes 10s-100s of kb away) regardless of orientation. They function by DNA looping that brings them into contact with the promoter via Mediator complex (Roger Kornberg 2006 Nobel). Tissue-specific enhancers explain why the same genome produces different cell types: hepatic enhancers drive albumin in liver; pancreatic enhancers drive insulin in β-cells. Mutations in enhancers cause disease: limb malformations (limb enhancer of Shh — preaxial polydactyly); fetal hemoglobin reactivation through enhancer disruption (BCL11A — basis of sickle-cell gene therapy approved 2023). AP Bio Unit 6 emphasizes enhancers as a major driver of tissue-specific gene regulation.

questions.prompt: Methylation of cytosine that silences gene expression heritably across cell divisions.
questions.explanation: DNA methylation (5-methylcytosine at CpG dinucleotides) is an epigenetic mark generally associated with gene silencing. ~70-80% of CpGs in vertebrates are methylated; unmethylated CpG islands (1-2 kb GC-rich regions, often at promoters) are typically associated with active genes. DNMTs add methyl groups; TET enzymes oxidize 5mC → 5hmC en route to demethylation. X-inactivation (Mary Lyon 1961) involves heavy methylation of the inactive X (Barr body). Genomic imprinting (parent-specific expression of ~100 human genes, e.g., IGF2 paternally expressed, H19 maternally; disorders include Prader-Willi and Angelman) uses methylation. Cancer often shows global hypomethylation + promoter hypermethylation of tumor suppressors. AP Bio Unit 6 + 4 link methylation to cell identity, development, and disease.

questions.prompt: CRISPR-Cas9 system targets and cuts DNA at sequences matching this RNA.
questions.explanation: The guide RNA (gRNA or single guide RNA, sgRNA) directs Cas9 endonuclease to a 20-bp DNA target adjacent to a PAM motif (NGG for SpCas9). Cas9 makes a blunt double-strand break that the cell repairs by NHEJ (error-prone, often introduces indels disrupting the gene — knockout) or HDR (homology-directed, if donor template provided — precise editing). Doudna and Charpentier (2020 Nobel) repurposed the bacterial adaptive immune system (CRISPR-Cas, Mojica 1990s, Barrangou-Horvath 2007) into a programmable editing tool in 2012. First CRISPR therapeutic Casgevy (exa-cel) for sickle cell + β-thalassemia, FDA approved Dec 2023 — disrupts BCL11A erythroid enhancer to reactivate fetal hemoglobin. AP Bio Unit 6 + 5 expect students to explain CRISPR mechanism and applications, including ethical questions of germline editing.

questions.prompt: Bacterial enzyme cutting DNA at specific palindromic sequences; foundational to cloning.
questions.explanation: Restriction enzymes (Type II) are bacterial endonucleases that cut DNA at specific palindromic recognition sequences (4-8 bp). Defense system against viral DNA (bacterial DNA is methylation-protected). Arber, Nathans, Smith (1978 Nobel) discovered and characterized them. EcoRI (5'-GAATTC-3', leaves sticky 5' overhangs), BamHI (GGATCC), HindIII (AAGCTT), EcoRV (GATATC, blunt). Make recombinant DNA: cut vector and insert with same enzyme → compatible sticky ends → ligate with DNA ligase. Foundation of molecular biology since the 1970s. Modern editing uses CRISPR-Cas9 (Doudna & Charpentier 2020 Nobel) or TALENs as programmable nucleases. AP Bio Unit 6 FRQs ask students to design cloning strategies using restriction maps.

questions.prompt: Lab technique separating DNA fragments by size in an electric field.
questions.explanation: Gel electrophoresis separates DNA by size: negatively charged DNA migrates toward the anode through agarose (or polyacrylamide for smaller fragments and proteins). Smaller fragments move farther. Visualization: ethidium bromide intercalation + UV (mutagenic, fading from labs); now SYBR Safe, GelRed. Used for restriction mapping, PCR product analysis, DNA fingerprinting (forensics — VNTRs/STRs after PCR), and Southern blotting. Sanger sequencing uses capillary electrophoresis to read 4 different ddNTP terminations. AP Bio Unit 6 FRQs require students to predict migration patterns based on fragment sizes and to interpret restriction digests via gel band positions — Science Practice 5 data analysis.

questions.prompt: Eukaryotic protein that binds promoter and recruits Pol II for transcription.
questions.explanation: Transcription factors (TFs) are sequence-specific DNA-binding proteins that activate or repress gene expression. Humans have ~1600 TFs. Categories: general TFs (TFIIA, B, D, E, F, H — assemble with Pol II at all promoters, form preinitiation complex); specific TFs (bind enhancers or silencers — Sp1, NF-κB, p53, Myc). TF DNA-binding domains include helix-turn-helix, zinc fingers (most common in humans), leucine zippers (Jun/Fos AP-1), basic helix-loop-helix (MyoD muscle determination). Mutations in TFs cause developmental disorders: HOX gene mutations alter body plans; FOXP3 mutations cause IPEX (immune dysfunction). AP Bio Unit 6 expects students to integrate TF binding with chromatin state and signal transduction.

questions.prompt: Plasmid carrying foreign DNA introduced into bacteria for replication.
questions.explanation: Plasmids are small (~3-10 kb) circular extrachromosomal DNA in bacteria, containing: origin of replication (ori), selectable marker (e.g., ampR antibiotic resistance), multiple cloning site (MCS with many restriction sites), often a promoter (T7, lac) for protein expression. Cohen and Boyer (1973) made the first recombinant plasmid (pSC101) — the birth of biotechnology. Insulin (Humulin, 1982) was the first recombinant therapeutic protein produced in E. coli. Selection: bacteria transformed with plasmid grow on amp plate; non-transformed die. Blue-white screening (lacZα disruption by insert → white colonies) identifies recombinants. AP Bio Unit 6 FRQs require students to design a cloning workflow from gene → plasmid → bacteria → expressed protein.

questions.prompt: Substitution of one base for another, potentially silent, missense, or nonsense.
questions.explanation: Point mutations are single-base substitutions: transitions (purine↔purine or pyrimidine↔pyrimidine, more common) and transversions (purine↔pyrimidine). Functional categories: silent (synonymous — same aa, often 3rd position wobble; ~25% of substitutions); missense (different aa — Glu6Val in HbS sickle cell); nonsense (sense → stop, truncating — DMD); regulatory (in promoter, enhancer, splice site). Indels cause frameshifts unless multiples of 3 (Δ508 in CFTR = in-frame, 3 nt deletion, but disrupts folding). Spontaneous mutation rates: ~10^-9 per bp per replication in humans → ~60 de novo mutations per person per generation (Conrad 2011 PNAS). AP Bio Unit 6 + 7 require predicting mutation consequences and evolutionary impact.

questions.prompt: Mode of selection where one extreme phenotype is favored, shifting the mean.
questions.explanation: Directional selection favors one phenotypic extreme, shifting the population mean over generations. Examples: peppered moth (Biston betularia) darkening during Industrial Revolution England as soot covered tree bark, then lightening after Clean Air Act 1956 (Kettlewell 1955 experiments); antibiotic resistance in bacteria (most rapid contemporary evolution — MRSA, multidrug TB); insecticide resistance (DDT in mosquitoes); beak depth in Galapagos medium ground finches (Grant and Grant 1973-present, 1.5 mm shift during 1977 drought favoring large seeds). AP Bio Unit 7 FRQs use directional selection examples as evidence of contemporary evolution — Science Practice 6 argumentation.

questions.prompt: Similar features in different species suggesting common ancestry, like vertebrate forelimbs.
questions.explanation: Homologous structures share evolutionary origin but may have different functions: tetrapod forelimbs (human arm, whale flipper, bat wing, horse hoof — same pentadactyl bone pattern, different uses). Distinct from analogous structures (similar function, different origin — bird wings vs. insect wings — convergent evolution from different starts). Vestigial structures (whale pelvic bones, human appendix, ostrich wings, snake leg buds) are reduced homologs supporting common descent. Modern evidence: shared developmental gene homology (HOX genes patterning all bilaterian body plans — Lewis 1995 Nobel for Drosophila genetics establishing this). AP Bio Unit 7 FRQs require students to distinguish homologous from analogous and use them to support phylogenetic claims.

questions.prompt: Universal genetic code, ribosomes, and cell membranes argue for this single origin.
questions.explanation: All known living organisms share a common ancestor — LUCA (Last Universal Common Ancestor) — based on multiple lines of evidence: nearly universal genetic code (same codons → aa); universal use of L-amino acids and D-sugars (chirality conservation); universal ATP energy currency; ribosomal architecture (16S/18S rRNA homology across domains); membrane lipid bilayers (though Archaea use isoprenoid ether-linked lipids, vs. Bacteria/Eukaryotes' fatty acid ester lipids); shared metabolic core (citric acid cycle, glycolysis). LUCA likely lived ~3.5-4.0 Bya as a thermophilic anaerobe. AP Bio Unit 7 expects students to integrate multiple molecular evidence lines into a common-ancestry argument — Science Practice 6 argumentation.

questions.prompt: Wing of bird, wing of bat, wing of insect — distinct origin, similar function.
questions.explanation: Convergent evolution: independent evolution of similar features in unrelated lineages facing similar selection pressures. Wings evolved separately in pterosaurs (Mesozoic reptiles), bats (mammals), birds (theropods), and insects (independently). Streamlined body in dolphins (mammals), tunas (fish), ichthyosaurs (reptiles) — fast aquatic predators. Camera-type eyes in vertebrates and cephalopods (octopus) developed independently with different retinal orientations (vertebrate retina is 'inverted' — photoreceptors face away from light). Echolocation in bats and toothed whales (with shared molecular convergence at prestin gene — Liu et al. 2010). Convergence supports adaptation as a powerful force shaping form. AP Bio Unit 7 FRQs use convergence as evidence that ENVIRONMENT shapes phenotype.

questions.prompt: Tempo and mode debate: long stasis punctuated by rapid speciation bursts.
questions.explanation: Punctuated equilibrium (Eldredge and Gould 1972) proposes that most species spend most of their existence in morphological stasis, punctuated by relatively brief (geologically — 5,000-50,000 yr) bursts of rapid change during speciation (often allopatric, in small peripheral populations). Contrasts with phyletic gradualism (Darwin's view: slow constant change). Fossil evidence: many species show stasis for millions of years, then sudden replacement by daughter species. Both modes occur — punctuated for some lineages, gradual for others. Punctuationism doesn't invoke new mechanisms — just different rates. AP Bio Unit 7 FRQs may ask students to interpret a phylogenetic diagram with branch lengths and identify stasis vs. rapid change.

questions.prompt: Equation predicting allele frequencies in a non-evolving population: p² + 2pq + q² = 1.
questions.explanation: Hardy (1908) and Weinberg (independently 1908) derived: for a two-allele locus (allele frequencies p + q = 1), genotype frequencies in a non-evolving population are p² (AA) + 2pq (Aa) + q² (aa) = 1. Assumes: (1) no mutation; (2) random mating; (3) no migration (gene flow); (4) infinite population (no drift); (5) no selection. Violation of ANY assumption causes evolution. The equation is a null model — deviations from H-W proportions diagnose evolutionary forces. AP Bio Unit 7 FRQs give phenotype counts (especially recessive q² visible directly), ask students to calculate p and q, then predict genotype frequencies — pure Science Practice 5 quantitative reasoning.

questions.prompt: Allele frequency of recessive disease allele if 1 in 10,000 has the recessive phenotype.
questions.explanation: If q² = 1/10,000 = 0.0001, then q = √0.0001 = 0.01. p = 1 - 0.01 = 0.99. Heterozygote frequency 2pq = 2 × 0.99 × 0.01 ≈ 0.02 = 1 in 50. This is the classic example of recessive disease genetics: cystic fibrosis (q² ≈ 1/2500 in Northern Europeans → q ≈ 0.02 → 2pq ≈ 4% carriers, ~1 in 25), Tay-Sachs (Ashkenazi Jewish population q ≈ 0.016 → 2pq ≈ 3.2% carriers), PKU (q² ≈ 1/10,000). Most disease alleles persist as recessives in carriers far outnumbering homozygotes. AP Bio Unit 7 FRQs commonly give an affected frequency and ask for carrier frequency — a frequent exam stem.

questions.prompt: Earliest known fossilized stromatolite-forming organisms, ~3.5 billion years old.
questions.explanation: Cyanobacteria (formerly 'blue-green algae') evolved oxygenic photosynthesis ~2.7-3.5 Bya. They built stromatolites — layered microbial mats (oldest 3.5 Bya from Australia, modern survivors in Shark Bay). The Great Oxidation Event (~2.4-2.0 Bya) was triggered by cyanobacterial O2 production, dramatically transforming Earth's atmosphere and oceans — banded iron formations record O2 rusting dissolved Fe(II). Later, an ancestor of modern chloroplasts (cyanobacterium) was engulfed in primary endosymbiosis (~1.5-2 Bya) producing the algal-plant lineage. AP Bio Unit 7 + 2 expect students to integrate cyanobacterial biology with endosymbiosis and atmospheric evolution — Science Practice 6 argumentation.

questions.prompt: Hardy-Weinberg condition broken when individuals choose mates with similar phenotypes.
questions.explanation: Random mating means individuals mate without regard to genotype/phenotype. Real populations often show ASSORTATIVE mating: positive (mate with similar — humans for height, IQ, ethnicity, age; increases homozygosity at relevant loci); negative (mate with different — MHC diversity in mice and humans, increases heterozygosity favorable for immune fitness). Disassortative MHC mating uses olfactory cues to detect MHC-different partners → more heterozygous offspring with broader immune repertoire. Inbreeding (mating with relatives) is an extreme form increasing homozygosity globally → inbreeding depression (recessive disease alleles homozygous). AP Bio Unit 7 FRQs ask students to predict deviations from H-W genotype frequencies when mating is non-random.

questions.prompt: Process where a small isolated population starts with limited allele diversity.
questions.explanation: Founder effect: a small population colonizing a new area carries only a subset of the parent population's allele diversity → strong genetic drift. Examples: Old Order Amish (Pennsylvania, ~200 founders) — high Ellis-van Creveld syndrome (EVC gene); Afrikaners (South African Dutch, ~200 founders ~1650) — porphyria variegata, Huntington's; Ashkenazi Jews (~350 founders after medieval bottlenecks) — Tay-Sachs, Gaucher, BRCA1/2 founder mutations. Bottleneck effect is similar (drastic population reduction by disaster — cheetahs, northern elephant seal post-hunting). Both reduce genetic variation and amplify rare alleles. AP Bio Unit 7 expects students to identify founder effects from population history clues.

questions.prompt: Mode of selection favoring intermediate phenotypes over extremes.
questions.explanation: Stabilizing selection favors the intermediate phenotype, reducing variance around the mean. Classic example: human birth weight — too low (~2.5 kg) and too high (~4.5 kg) both have higher infant mortality; intermediate (3.0-3.5 kg) has lowest mortality (Karn and Penrose 1951 study). Other examples: clutch size in birds (David Lack hypothesis — intermediate clutches maximize fledged offspring); gall size in goldenrod (small parasitized by wasps, large pecked by birds, intermediate survives best). Distribution narrows around the mean. AP Bio Unit 7 FRQs give phenotype distributions before/after selection and ask students to classify as stabilizing, directional, or disruptive (Science Practice 5 graph reading).

questions.prompt: Mode of selection favoring both extremes simultaneously, potentially creating speciation.
questions.explanation: Disruptive (diversifying) selection favors BOTH extremes, selecting against intermediates → bimodal distribution. May initiate sympatric speciation. Examples: African seedcracker finch (Pyrenestes ostrinus) — large-billed birds eat hard seeds, small-billed eat soft seeds, intermediate-billed do neither well (Smith 1993 Nature); apple maggot fly (Rhagoletis pomonella) host race shift after introduction of apples 150 years ago (host preferences disruptively selecting genotypes); Darwin's finches under fluctuating drought-rainfall conditions favoring different beak morphs. AP Bio Unit 7 expects students to recognize disruptive selection from bimodal phenotype data and connect it to speciation potential.

questions.prompt: Selection on mating traits like peacock tails, including male-male and female choice.
questions.explanation: Sexual selection (Darwin 1871 'Descent of Man') drives traits that enhance mating success at the cost of survival. Intrasexual (male-male combat: elk antlers, elephant seal weight, gorilla canines) and intersexual (female choice: peacock tails, bird-of-paradise plumage, bowerbird nests). Fisher's runaway model (1930): females preferring a trait + heritable preference → exponential trait elaboration. Zahavi's handicap principle (1975): exaggerated traits are honest signals because only fit individuals can afford them. Hamilton-Zuk hypothesis (1982): bright males indicate parasite resistance. Sexual dimorphism (male-female differences) results. AP Bio Unit 7 + 8 link sexual selection to phenotype maintenance even when seemingly maladaptive for survival.

questions.prompt: Probability gain or loss of an allele due purely to chance in finite populations.
questions.explanation: Genetic drift is random change in allele frequencies due to sampling error in finite populations. Effect inversely proportional to effective population size (Ne): small populations drift fast (alleles fix or are lost quickly); large populations drift slowly. Wright (1931) developed mathematical theory. Drift is non-selective — neutral or even beneficial alleles can be lost. Neutral theory of molecular evolution (Kimura 1968): most substitutions at the molecular level are selectively neutral and reach fixation by drift. Effective population size of humans was ~10,000 for most of history — small enough that drift shaped much variation. AP Bio Unit 7 FRQs ask students to compare drift vs. selection roles in small vs. large populations.

questions.prompt: Phenomenon where sickle-cell heterozygotes (Hb AS) are favored in malaria zones.
questions.explanation: Heterozygote advantage (overdominance): heterozygotes have higher fitness than either homozygote, maintaining BOTH alleles in the population (balancing selection). Classic example: sickle-cell anemia in malaria-endemic Africa (Allison 1954) — AA homozygotes susceptible to fatal P. falciparum malaria; SS homozygotes have lethal sickle-cell disease; AS heterozygotes have mild trait + 90% malaria resistance (sickled RBCs containing parasites are cleared faster). HbS frequency 10-30% in malaria zones. Similar logic for thalassemia (Mediterranean malaria zones), G6PD deficiency, Duffy-negative blood (resists P. vivax). MHC heterozygosity also balanced (broader pathogen recognition). AP Bio Unit 7 FRQs ask students to predict allele frequencies under balancing selection — disrupts simple H-W expectations.

questions.prompt: Biological species concept defining species by reproductive isolation.
questions.explanation: Biological species concept (BSC, Mayr 1942): a species is a group of populations whose members can interbreed and produce fertile offspring AND are reproductively isolated from other species. Reproductive isolation can be prezygotic (habitat, temporal, behavioral, mechanical, gametic) or postzygotic (hybrid inviability, sterility, breakdown — F2 weakness). Limitations: doesn't apply to asexual species (bacteria — use phylogenetic + ecological species concepts); fossils (use morphological species concept); ring species (Ensatina salamanders); hybridization is common in plants and many animals. AP Bio Unit 7 expects students to evaluate species concepts critically — Science Practice 6 argumentation.

questions.prompt: Speciation through geographic isolation, e.g., the Galapagos finches.
questions.explanation: Allopatric (Greek: 'other place') speciation occurs when populations are geographically separated, accumulate divergent mutations through drift + selection + recombination, and lose interbreeding capability. Examples: Galapagos finches (~15 species from one mainland ancestor, ~2-3 Mya); Hawaiian Drosophila (>800 species after island formation 5 Mya); cichlid fish in African Great Lakes (~1000 species in Lake Victoria after Pleistocene glaciation drained and refilled the lake ~14,000 years ago — explosive radiation); Isthmus of Panama closing ~3 Mya separated Atlantic + Pacific marine sister species. Vicariance vs. dispersal events both work. AP Bio Unit 7 FRQs use island biogeography (MacArthur-Wilson 1967) as a fertile setup for speciation questions.

questions.prompt: Speciation without geographic isolation, often via polyploidy or disruptive selection.
questions.explanation: Sympatric (Greek: 'same place') speciation occurs in overlapping geographic ranges. Mechanisms: (1) polyploidy (sudden chromosome doubling reproductively isolates polyploid offspring from diploid parents in ONE generation — common in plants: ~80% of flowering plant species are polyploid; wheat is hexaploid, cotton is tetraploid); (2) disruptive selection on resource use (apple maggot fly Rhagoletis — host race shift; cichlid fish on different lake habitats); (3) sexual selection (assortative mating on display traits — Lake Victoria cichlids before light pollution from human runoff disrupted color-based mate choice). AP Bio Unit 7 expects students to contrast allopatric vs. sympatric mechanisms and identify which fits a given scenario.

questions.prompt: Reproductive barriers preventing fertilization between species (e.g., temporal, behavioral).
questions.explanation: Prezygotic isolation prevents fertilization. Types: (1) habitat isolation (garter snakes — terrestrial vs. aquatic); (2) temporal isolation (eastern + western spotted skunks breed at different times; bamboo flowering cycles spanning decades); (3) behavioral isolation (firefly flash patterns species-specific; bird songs); (4) mechanical isolation (incompatible genitalia in insects, plants pollinated by different-sized insects); (5) gametic isolation (sea urchin sperm protein bindin only binds same-species egg receptor; pollen tubes fail to grow in wrong-species styles). All these prevent gene flow before zygote formation. Postzygotic barriers (hybrid inviability — Drosophila species hybrids die; sterility — mules from horse × donkey; breakdown — F2 weakness) act AFTER zygote formation. AP Bio Unit 7 FRQs distinguish these.

questions.prompt: Rapid burst of speciation as one lineage diversifies into many niches.
questions.explanation: Adaptive radiation: rapid diversification of one lineage into many species adapted to different niches. Triggers: ecological opportunity (new habitat, mass extinction, evolutionary innovation). Examples: Galapagos finches (Darwin's finches, ~15 species from common ancestor ~2-3 Mya, beak morphology matching food sources); Hawaiian honeycreepers (>50 species from one founder); cichlid fish in Lake Victoria (~500 species in <100,000 years — fastest known radiation); Cambrian explosion (~540 Mya — major animal phyla appeared in ~20-30 Myr after oxygen rose and predator-prey arms races began); placental mammal radiation after K-Pg extinction 66 Mya (cleared niches). Cause + effect: ecological opportunity + key innovation → rapid speciation. AP Bio Unit 7 + 8 integrate this with ecology and biogeography.

questions.prompt: Darwin's 1859 book proposing descent with modification and natural selection.
questions.explanation: Charles Darwin's On the Origin of Species (1859) proposed two unifying ideas: (1) all life shares common ancestry (descent with modification); (2) natural selection is the mechanism of adaptive change. Built on 1831-1836 HMS Beagle voyage observations (Galapagos finches, mockingbirds, tortoises showing island variation; Lyell's Principles of Geology providing deep time). Alfred Russel Wallace independently proposed natural selection in 1858 — they co-published. Darwin lacked a mechanism for heredity (Mendel was a contemporary but unknown); 'modern synthesis' (Dobzhansky, Mayr, Simpson 1937-1944) integrated Mendelian genetics with Darwinism. AP Bio Unit 7 expects students to articulate Darwin's logic: variation + inheritance + differential reproduction → adaptive evolution.

questions.prompt: 1953 experiment producing organic molecules from reduced atmosphere and lightning.
questions.explanation: Stanley Miller (Urey lab, 1953) sealed CH4, NH3, H2O, H2 (mimicking early reducing atmosphere) and a spark discharge (lightning) for one week. Produced glycine, alanine, and several other amino acids spontaneously — first experimental support for abiotic prebiotic chemistry (Oparin 1924, Haldane 1929 'primordial soup' hypothesis). Subsequent experiments (Bada et al. 2008) reanalyzed Miller's original samples with modern HPLC-MS and found 20+ amino acids. Other prebiotic sources: hydrothermal vents (Wächtershäuser pyrite metabolism), space (amino acids on Murchison meteorite, 1969). RNA world hypothesis (Gilbert 1986): RNA as both information carrier + catalyst preceded DNA/protein world. AP Bio Unit 7 FRQs require students to evaluate evidence for abiotic origin.

questions.prompt: Field of biology mapping evolutionary relationships through tree diagrams.
questions.explanation: Phylogenetics reconstructs evolutionary relationships using morphological, behavioral, and (predominantly) molecular data. Cladograms show branching patterns based on shared derived characters (synapomorphies — Hennig 1966 cladistic methodology). Phylogenetic trees include branch lengths proportional to genetic distance or time. Methods: maximum parsimony (Occam's razor — fewest changes), maximum likelihood, Bayesian inference. Modern phylogenetics uses multi-locus + whole-genome data with software (BEAST, RAxML, MrBayes). Tree of life: three domains (Bacteria, Archaea, Eukarya — Woese 1977 16S rRNA-based). AP Bio Unit 7 FRQs commonly give an alignment or character matrix and ask students to construct a tree, identify monophyletic groups, and infer ancestral states.

questions.prompt: Sudden ~540 Mya emergence of most animal phyla in the fossil record.
questions.explanation: The Cambrian explosion (~540-520 Mya) is the rapid appearance of most modern animal phyla in the fossil record over ~20-30 Myr — preserved spectacularly in Burgess Shale (Cambrian shale, British Columbia; Walcott 1909 discovery, Stephen Jay Gould's 'Wonderful Life'), Chengjiang (China), Sirius Passet (Greenland). Hypotheses for the burst: rising O2 levels (enabling large active animals); evolution of Hox genes + body-plan diversification; predator-prey arms race; Ediacaran biota wipeout; ecological opportunity. Hard parts (exoskeletons, shells) appeared (taphonomic bias toward preservation). AP Bio Unit 7 expects students to evaluate explanatory hypotheses with multiple-causation reasoning.

questions.prompt: Mass extinction at the K-Pg boundary that killed non-avian dinosaurs 66 Mya.
questions.explanation: The K-Pg (formerly K-T) extinction ~66 Mya killed ~76% of species, including all non-avian dinosaurs, pterosaurs, ammonites, mosasaurs, and most marine plankton. Cause: Chicxulub asteroid impact (~10 km diameter, Yucatán crater 180 km wide) + Deccan Traps volcanism in India. Alvarez et al. (1980) discovered the global Ir-enriched K-Pg boundary layer — extraterrestrial origin since asteroids are Ir-rich. Survivors: small mammals, birds (avian dinosaurs), crocodilians, turtles. Mass extinction cleared niches, enabling mammal + bird adaptive radiation through the Cenozoic. Five mass extinctions: end-Ordovician (~444 Mya), late Devonian (~375), end-Permian (~252, the 'Great Dying', ~96% marine species lost), end-Triassic (~201), end-Cretaceous (~66). AP Bio Unit 7 + 8 link mass extinctions to evolutionary radiation.

questions.prompt: Population growth model with constant per capita rate, producing J-shaped curve.
questions.explanation: Exponential growth: dN/dt = rN, where N = population size, r = intrinsic per capita growth rate. Produces a J-shaped curve. Occurs when resources are abundant and density-independent (early colonization, after bottlenecks, lab cultures during log phase). Malthus (1798) predicted exponential human growth would exceed linear food supply → famine; Darwin extended this to all populations. Doubling time = ln(2)/r ≈ 0.693/r. Human population reached 8 billion in 2022 with growth rate ~0.9%/yr (doubling time ~77 yr — slowing). Bacteria can divide every 20 min in optimal conditions → one cell → 4.7 × 10^21 in 24 h (limited by resources before this). AP Bio Unit 8 FRQs use exponential calculation on graphed data.

questions.prompt: Population model: dN/dt = rN((K-N)/K), producing S-shaped curve.
questions.explanation: Logistic growth: dN/dt = rN × (K-N)/K. Verhulst (1838) introduced; rediscovered by Pearl and Reed (1920). Produces S-shaped (sigmoidal) curve: early exponential when N << K, slowing at inflection point N = K/2 (maximum dN/dt), plateau at K. Models density-dependent regulation (competition, predation, disease intensify as N increases). Real-world fits: barnacle larvae settlement, sheep populations on Tasmania, yeast in lab cultures, sea otters recovering after hunting bans. AP Bio Unit 8 FRQs require students to identify N = K/2 (steepest growth), interpret asymptotic K, and contrast with exponential — frequent Science Practice 5 quantitative question.

questions.prompt: Population type with many small offspring and high mortality — fish, insects, weeds.
questions.explanation: R-selected (r-strategist) species: high r (intrinsic growth rate), many small offspring, little parental care, short generation time, high mortality, density-INdependent regulation often, opportunistic colonizers of disturbed habitats. Examples: mosquitoes, fish, dandelions, mice, ruderal plants, bacteria. K-selected: large body, few large offspring, parental care, long-lived, density-DEpendent regulation, stable habitats. Examples: elephants, whales, oaks, humans, gorillas, redwoods. MacArthur and Wilson (1967) island biogeography theory introduced r/K dichotomy. Most species lie on a continuum; pure types are extremes. AP Bio Unit 8 FRQs ask students to predict population dynamics from life-history descriptions.

questions.prompt: Climate change effect causing phenological shifts in many species.
questions.explanation: Climate change causes species range shifts (poleward and upslope) and phenological shifts (earlier spring events: budding, hatching, migration). Average ~17 km poleward + 11 m uphill per decade (Parmesan and Yohe 2003 Nature). Phenological mismatch: if predator and prey, or pollinator and flower, shift at DIFFERENT rates, their interaction breaks down. Pied flycatcher in Netherlands: lays eggs 10 days earlier than 30 years ago but not enough to match earlier oak caterpillar peak (its food) → 90% population decline (Both et al. 2006). Polar bears + sea ice loss reducing seal hunting; coral bleaching above 31°C threatening tropical reefs. AP Bio Unit 8 + 7 require students to predict ecological consequences of climate change with quantitative reasoning.

questions.prompt: Habitat patch theory predicting species richness from area and isolation.
questions.explanation: Island biogeography (MacArthur and Wilson 1967 book) predicts species richness from immigration (decreases with isolation) + extinction (increases with smaller area) → equilibrium S = cA^z (Arrhenius-Preston species-area relationship; z typically 0.15-0.4 for islands). Applies to true islands AND habitat fragments (forest patches, parks, mountaintops — sky islands). SLOSS debate (Single Large Or Several Small reserves) addresses optimal conservation design. Habitat fragmentation reduces effective patch size + increases edge effects → smaller, more isolated populations more vulnerable to extinction. Wilson and Simberloff's mangrove defaunation experiments (1969-70) experimentally confirmed the theory. AP Bio Unit 8 expects students to apply species-area logic to conservation problems.

questions.prompt: Two species needing the same limiting resource compete; closely related species often.
questions.explanation: Competition (-/-) for limiting resources (food, water, light, space, mates). Intraspecific (within species — driver of logistic regulation) vs. interspecific (between species). Gause's competitive exclusion principle (1934): two species cannot coexist indefinitely on the same limiting resource — one outcompetes the other (extinction or niche shift). Niche partitioning allows coexistence: MacArthur's warblers (1958) — 5 species coexist on spruce trees by feeding in different zones. Character displacement: traits diverge in sympatry to reduce competition (Galapagos finches' beak sizes diverge on shared islands). AP Bio Unit 8 expects students to predict outcomes from competition coefficients and to identify resource partitioning.

questions.prompt: Behavior pattern in which a young animal forms attachment during sensitive period.
questions.explanation: Imprinting: rapid, durable learning during a sensitive (critical) period in early life, often without conventional reinforcement. Konrad Lorenz (1935, Nobel 1973) showed greylag goslings imprint on the first moving object seen post-hatching — typically the mother, but Lorenz himself substituted in famous photos. Filial imprinting (offspring → parent — guides early following), sexual imprinting (mate choice based on parental phenotype). Sensitive period in geese is ~13-16 hr post-hatching. Whooping crane conservation uses costumed handlers + ultralight aircraft to imprint chicks on migration route without permanent attachment. AP Bio Unit 8 distinguishes imprinting from associative learning and habituation.

questions.prompt: Species whose impact disproportionately exceeds its abundance, e.g., sea otters.
questions.explanation: Keystone species (Paine 1966 — coined the term studying Pisaster ochraceus sea stars on Washington coast) have disproportionate effect relative to their abundance. Removal causes cascading community changes. Classic examples: sea stars (preying on mussels prevents mussel dominance — without sea stars, intertidal becomes monoculture); sea otters (eating sea urchins protects kelp forests — without otters, urchin barrens replace kelp); wolves in Yellowstone (1995 reintroduction reduced elk → recovered willow + aspen → recovered beaver + songbirds → trophic cascade); beavers (engineer wetlands); coral, kelp, mangroves (foundation species — large physical effect). AP Bio Unit 8 FRQs require students to predict community effects of keystone loss.

questions.prompt: Plot of mortality by age, with three classic shapes (I, II, III).
questions.explanation: Survivorship curves plot log(number alive) vs. age. Three types: Type I — low juvenile mortality, high old-age mortality (concave from above; humans, elephants, large mammals, K-selected); Type II — constant mortality across ages (linear log-scale; birds, small mammals, lizards, hydra); Type III — high juvenile mortality, low adult mortality (convex from above; oysters releasing millions of larvae, oak trees with acorns, fish, r-selected). Constructed from life tables tracking cohort survival. Deevey (1947) developed the classification. AP Bio Unit 8 FRQs give a survivorship plot and ask students to classify the species' life history strategy and predict consequences for population dynamics — Science Practice 5 data analysis.

questions.prompt: Loss of vegetation and ecosystem due to overhunting at multiple levels above.
questions.explanation: Trophic cascades are top-down effects of predators on lower trophic levels. Yellowstone wolf reintroduction (1995): wolves reduced elk browsing → willows + aspens recovered → beavers (eat willow) returned → wetlands restored → songbirds, fish, amphibians returned (William Ripple research). Sea otters → urchin reduction → kelp forest recovery (kelp = 'underwater rainforests' supporting many species). Without top predators, herbivores explode, vegetation collapses — classic 'ecosystem of fear' studies (Estes, Brashares). Marine cod overfishing → urchin explosion → kelp loss (Atlantic Canada). AP Bio Unit 8 + 7 link trophic cascades to conservation biology and the consequences of mesopredator release.

questions.prompt: Total organic compound produced by photosynthesis per area per time.
questions.explanation: Primary productivity is the rate of biomass production by primary producers (photosynthesizers). GPP = gross primary productivity (total photosynthesis); NPP = net primary productivity (GPP minus producer respiration) — what's available for consumers. Units: g C/m²/yr or kJ/m²/yr. Global NPP ~105 Gt C/yr, split ~50/50 between terrestrial (tropical forests highest at ~2200 g/m²/yr) and marine (open ocean low at ~125 g/m²/yr; coastal upwellings much higher). Limited by light (intensity, latitude, depth), nutrients (N in temperate, P in tropical, Fe in oligotrophic ocean — Martin's iron hypothesis), temperature, water. AP Bio Unit 8 FRQs ask students to interpret biome NPP graphs and identify limiting factors.

questions.prompt: Rule: ~10% of energy passes from one trophic level to the next.
questions.explanation: The 10% rule (Lindeman 1942) states only ~10% of energy at one trophic level is incorporated into the next; ~90% is lost as heat (entropy from cellular respiration), motion, undigested matter (feces), and dead biomass. Consequences: short food chains (typically 3-5 levels max — energy runs out); top predators are rare; eating lower on the food chain is more energetically efficient (1 kg beef requires ~10 kg feed). Actual efficiencies vary 1-25% (ectotherms more efficient than endotherms; aquatic systems often higher than terrestrial). Lindeman drowned at age 27 just after submitting the foundational 1942 ecology paper. AP Bio Unit 8 FRQs use 10% rule to calculate maximum biomass at top of food chain.

questions.prompt: Two species both benefit from the interaction, like clownfish and anemone.
questions.explanation: Mutualism (+/+): both species benefit. Examples: mycorrhizae (fungi + plant roots — fungi get sugars, plant gets P/N + larger absorption surface; ~90% of plants); rhizobia (N2-fixing bacteria + legume roots in root nodules — leghemoglobin protects nitrogenase from O2); pollination (flower + bee/bird/bat); coral + zooxanthellae (algae provide ~90% of coral energy via photosynthesis); cleaner fish + reef fish; lichens (fungi + alga/cyanobacteria — totally interdependent). Gut microbiome in humans: ~10^13-10^14 bacterial cells, mostly mutualistic — provide vitamins (K, biotin, folate), short-chain fatty acids, immune training. AP Bio Unit 8 FRQs use mutualism examples to illustrate coevolution and ecosystem function.

questions.prompt: Diagram showing energy flow and number/biomass at each trophic level.
questions.explanation: Ecological pyramids quantify trophic structure. Energy pyramid (always upright due to 10% rule — energy decreases up). Biomass pyramid (usually upright; inverted in marine systems where phytoplankton turn over rapidly — small standing stock supports large fish biomass via high productivity). Number pyramid (can be inverted if one tree supports many insects). Elton (1927) introduced pyramid concept. Modeling: at 10% efficiency, 10,000 kg producers → 1,000 kg primary consumer → 100 kg secondary → 10 kg tertiary. AP Bio Unit 8 FRQs give producer NPP and ask students to calculate energy available at top — pure Science Practice 5 quantitative reasoning.

questions.prompt: Bottom layer of food web: photosynthetic and chemosynthetic organisms.
questions.explanation: Producers (autotrophs) fix inorganic carbon (CO2) into organic compounds. Photoautotrophs use light energy: plants, algae, cyanobacteria, purple/green sulfur bacteria. Chemoautotrophs use chemical energy: hydrothermal vent bacteria oxidize H2S → produce sugar (~50 species of vent organisms depend on these microbes, not sunlight — discovered 1977 at Galapagos Rift, fundamentally changed biology by showing ecosystems can exist without sunlight). Nitrifying bacteria (Nitrosomonas, Nitrobacter) oxidize NH3 → NO2 → NO3-. Iron-oxidizing bacteria in acid mine drainage. AP Bio Unit 8 + 3 FRQs ask students to compare photosynthesis-based vs. chemosynthesis-based ecosystems and to predict resilience under climate change.

questions.prompt: Two species interaction where one benefits and one is harmed; e.g., lion and zebra.
questions.explanation: Predation (+/-): predator kills and eats prey. Distinguish from parasitism (parasite lives in/on host, usually doesn't kill quickly) and herbivory (eating plants — often doesn't kill the plant). Predator-prey dynamics modeled by Lotka-Volterra equations (1925-26): coupled oscillations with predator lagging prey. Classic data: Hudson Bay Company lynx + snowshoe hare 10-yr cycles from fur trapping records (Elton 1942 — early ecology). Coevolutionary arms races: cheetah speed vs. gazelle speed; rough-skinned newt tetrodotoxin vs. garter snake resistance. AP Bio Unit 8 FRQs interpret predator-prey graphs and identify lag, amplitude, and stability. The Lotka-Volterra model has limits — assumes constant rates.

questions.prompt: Maximum population size an environment can sustainably support.
questions.explanation: Carrying capacity (K) is the maximum population an environment can sustain indefinitely given resource limits (food, water, space, predators, disease). When N approaches K, growth slows due to intraspecific competition. Variable: changes with weather, season, resource availability. Estimates of human K range 7-15 billion (controversial — depends on technology, consumption assumptions). Real populations often oscillate around K (overshoot + crash cycles common in lemmings, snowshoe hares, and many insects). AP Bio Unit 8 FRQs ask students to estimate K from logistic curves (where dN/dt = 0) — Science Practice 5 graph reading.

questions.prompt: Greenhouse gas + macronutrient cycle accelerated by fossil fuel burning.
questions.explanation: Carbon cycles between atmospheric CO2 (~420 ppm currently, up from 280 ppm pre-industrial), oceans (dissolved CO2 + bicarbonate — largest active reservoir), biosphere (NPP fixes ~120 Gt C/yr), and geological reservoirs (limestone, fossil fuels, methane hydrates — slowest). Photosynthesis pulls CO2 from atmosphere; respiration + combustion + decomposition return it. Fossil fuel burning releases ~10 Gt C/yr (5× pre-industrial geological release rate); deforestation adds ~1-2 Gt C/yr. Half stays in atmosphere; ~1/4 ocean uptake (causing acidification); ~1/4 land sinks. Keeling curve (Mauna Loa 1958-present, Charles Keeling) directly measures atmospheric CO2 rise. AP Bio Unit 8 expects students to trace C through the cycle and predict effects of perturbations.

questions.prompt: Cycle limited mostly to lithosphere-biosphere exchange, lacking gaseous phase.
questions.explanation: The P cycle is mostly lithospheric — phosphorus has no significant gaseous phase. Weathering of phosphate rocks releases PO4³- → soil → uptake by plants → consumers → death/excretion → decomposers regenerate → runoff to oceans → marine sediments → uplift back to rocks (geological timescale). P is the most common limiting nutrient in tropical soils and freshwater. Agricultural mining (Florida phosphate beds, Morocco's reserves — over 70% of world's known P) is finite — 'peak phosphorus' may occur this century. Eutrophication (excess P from agricultural runoff → algal blooms → dead zones like Gulf of Mexico, Lake Erie, Chesapeake Bay) is a major P-cycle disruption. AP Bio Unit 8 FRQs ask students to compare gaseous vs. sedimentary biogeochemical cycles.

questions.prompt: Bacteria, fungi, and other organisms breaking down dead matter, returning nutrients.
questions.explanation: Decomposers (bacteria, fungi — saprotrophs) and detritivores (earthworms, millipedes, dung beetles, vultures, sea cucumbers) break down dead organisms and waste, releasing nutrients (N, P, S, C) back to the abiotic pool for producer reuse. Without them, dead matter would accumulate and biogeochemical cycles would stall within ~1 yr (Carboniferous Period 359-299 Mya: lignin in early woody plants couldn't be decomposed — coal deposits resulted before white-rot fungi evolved to degrade lignin ~290 Mya). Fungi are particularly important woody-tissue decomposers. Detritivores process leaf litter, dung, and carcasses. AP Bio Unit 8 expects students to identify decomposers as energy DOES flow through them (mostly to heat) but their role is primarily in NUTRIENT CYCLING.

questions.prompt: Cycle moving atmospheric N2 into biological molecules via specialized bacteria.
questions.explanation: The N cycle: atmospheric N2 (78% of air, inert triple bond) → fixed N (NH3, NH4+) by nitrogen-fixing bacteria (Rhizobium in legume nodules, Azotobacter free-living, cyanobacteria, lightning) → nitrification by Nitrosomonas (NH3 → NO2-) and Nitrobacter (NO2- → NO3-) → uptake by plants → consumers → death/excretion → ammonification by decomposers (back to NH3) → denitrification (Pseudomonas, anaerobic, NO3- → N2 + N2O — closes cycle). Haber-Bosch process (1909, Nobel 1918 for Haber) industrially fixes N2 at high T/P with Fe catalyst — supports ~50% of humanity (fertilizer); huge environmental impact (eutrophication, dead zones, N2O greenhouse gas). AP Bio Unit 8 + 7 emphasize the disproportionate human role in N cycle.

questions.prompt: Innate stereotyped behavior triggered by a sign stimulus.
questions.explanation: Fixed action patterns (FAPs) are innate, stereotyped, complete-once-triggered behaviors initiated by sign stimuli (releasers). Tinbergen's classic studies (1951, Nobel 1973 with Lorenz, von Frisch): male stickleback attacks anything with red belly (releaser = red); herring gull chick pecks at red spot on parent beak to elicit feeding regurgitation; geese roll any egg-like object back to nest (egg-rolling FAP). FAPs require no learning, are species-specific, and are crucial for survival in stable environments. Once triggered, run to completion even if disturbed. AP Bio Unit 8 expects students to distinguish FAPs (genetic) from learned behavior (associative learning, imprinting, habituation) — Science Practice 6 argumentation.

questions.prompt: Behavior reducing fitness of doer while increasing fitness of recipient.
questions.explanation: Altruism: behavior that REDUCES the actor's reproductive fitness while INCREASING another's. Hamilton's rule (1964): altruism evolves when rB > C, where r = coefficient of relatedness, B = benefit to recipient, C = cost to actor. Kin selection explains most cases: helping siblings (r=0.5), nieces/nephews (r=0.25). Inclusive fitness includes direct (own offspring) + indirect (kin offspring helped) reproduction. Examples: vampire bat food sharing (with non-kin too — reciprocal altruism, Trivers 1971); meerkat sentinels (r ≈ 0.32 in family groups); naked mole rat eusociality (extremely high relatedness through inbreeding). Reciprocal altruism (delayed payback) and group selection are also proposed mechanisms. AP Bio Unit 8 + 7 expects evolutionary explanations of cooperation.

questions.prompt: Hydrologic process moving water from earth to atmosphere and back.
questions.explanation: The water cycle moves H2O between ocean (97.5% — saline), polar ice/glaciers (1.7%), groundwater (0.76%), surface freshwater (0.013%), atmosphere (0.001%), and biota. Processes: evaporation (oceans + lakes), transpiration (plants — ~10% of atmospheric H2O comes from plants), condensation, precipitation, runoff, infiltration. Mean atmospheric residence time ~9 days. Energy: solar drives evaporation; latent heat releases on condensation. Climate change intensifies the cycle (warmer atmosphere holds more water vapor — 7% per °C, Clausius-Clapeyron) → more droughts AND more intense storms/flooding. Glaciers and snowpack are major freshwater stores. AP Bio Unit 8 expects students to link the water cycle to climate, ecosystems, and human water security.

questions.prompt: Process by which marine cyanobacteria sequester atmospheric carbon via shells.
questions.explanation: The biological pump transfers atmospheric CO2 to deep ocean via marine photosynthesis: phytoplankton fix CO2 → eaten by zooplankton → fecal pellets/dead bodies sink → some buried in sediments (carbonate platforms, coal-equivalent oil) → effectively removed from atmosphere on geological timescales. Calcifying plankton (coccolithophores, foraminifera) sink as CaCO3 (alkalinity pump). Without the biological pump, atmospheric CO2 would be ~50% higher. Ocean acidification (CO2 + H2O → H2CO3 → H+ + HCO3-) threatens calcifying organisms — shells dissolve below aragonite saturation horizon, which is rising as ocean pH drops from 8.2 (1850) to 8.1 (2024) — a 30% increase in [H+]. AP Bio Unit 8 + 7 link this to climate change and conservation.

questions.prompt: Energy currency made by oxidative phosphorylation and consumed by all cellular work.
questions.explanation: ATP unifies Units 1 (structure: adenine + ribose + 3 phosphates), 3 (synthesis: chemiosmosis in mito + chloroplasts; Mitchell 1978 Nobel), 4 (signaling: cAMP from ATP; kinases use ATP), 5+6 (DNA replication; transcription with ATP precursor; ATP-dependent chromatin remodeling), 7 (origin of life: RNA-world predecessors). Cell turnover ~50 kg/day from ~50 g standing pool. ATP synthase rotational mechanism (Boyer-Walker 1997 Nobel). AP Bio: students must trace ATP across all 8 units — a frequent multi-unit synthesis FRQ. ΔG ≈ -30.5 kJ/mol on hydrolysis; ~-50 kJ/mol in cellular conditions.

questions.prompt: Process unifying Units 4 and 6: regulated dismantling that recycles biomolecules.
questions.explanation: Apoptosis links Units 4 (cell-cycle control + caspase cascades), 6 (transcriptional regulation of Bax/Bcl-2/Puma by p53), 7 (evolution — discovered in C. elegans developmental death, Brenner-Horvitz-Sulston 2002 Nobel), 8 (tissue homeostasis + immune T cell selection). Intrinsic (mitochondrial — Bax/Bak permeabilization → cytochrome c → apoptosome → caspase-9 → caspase-3) vs. extrinsic (death receptor → caspase-8 → caspase-3) pathways. Defective apoptosis in cancer (Bcl-2 t(14;18) in follicular lymphoma — Korsmeyer 1985 first survival oncogene). AP Bio cross-unit FRQs use apoptosis to integrate signaling + transcription + evolution.

questions.prompt: Iconic 1953 experiment producing amino acids from a reducing atmosphere + spark.
questions.explanation: Miller-Urey (1953) — abiotic synthesis of amino acids — links Units 1 (macromolecules), 7 (origin of life). Reanalysis of original samples (Bada 2008) found 20+ amino acids. RNA world hypothesis (Gilbert 1986) extends this: RNA as both catalyst (ribozymes — Cech-Altman 1989 Nobel) + information carrier predates DNA + protein world. Modern panspermia variants: amino acids on Murchison meteorite, ribose detected in carbonaceous chondrites (Furukawa 2019). Discovery of life-relevant chemistry on Saturn's Titan + Mars + Enceladus actively studied by NASA. AP Bio expects students to evaluate evidence + design replications — Science Practice 4.

questions.prompt: Molecule storing genetic information across all three domains of life.
questions.explanation: DNA unifies Units 1 (nucleotide chemistry, antiparallel double helix — Watson-Crick 1953), 5 (Mendelian inheritance via chromosomes — Sutton-Boveri 1903), 6 (replication, transcription, regulation, biotechnology), 7 (evolution: phylogenetic comparisons, mutation as variation source, ancient DNA recovery from Neanderthals — Pääbo 2022 Nobel).
