Universal genetic code (Nirenberg 1968 Nobel) supports common ancestry (Unit 7). AP Bio expects students to integrate DNA's information-storage, replication, expression, and evolutionary roles.

questions.prompt: Two-step process: DNA → mRNA, then mRNA → protein.
questions.explanation: Crick's central dogma (1958) links Units 1 (macromolecules), 4 (regulated gene expression in signaling), 5 (inheritance — what's encoded), 6 (transcription + translation), 7 (evolution at the molecular level). Apparent exceptions extend the dogma: retroviruses (RNA→DNA via reverse transcriptase — Baltimore + Temin 1975 Nobel; basis of HIV biology + retrotransposon evolution); prions (protein → protein conformational templating — Prusiner 1997 Nobel; NOT heritable through nucleic acid). Crick anticipated reverse transcription as a footnote. AP Bio expects all students to fluently navigate the dogma across course units.

questions.prompt: Process linking Units 2 and 3 across membranes via electrochemical gradients.
questions.explanation: Chemiosmosis (Mitchell 1961, Nobel 1978) unifies Units 2 (membrane structure — H+ impermeable bilayer, transmembrane ATP synthase), 3 (photosynthesis + respiration both use H+ gradients), 7 (deep evolutionary conservation — F-ATP synthase architecture conserved from bacteria to humans). Same mechanism, same enzyme architecture, different membranes: thylakoid (lumen acidic) vs. inner mitochondrial (intermembrane space acidic). Uncouplers (DNP) collapse gradient, killing ATP synthesis (used as diet drug 1930s, lethal). Brown adipose tissue intentionally uncouples (UCP1) for heat. AP Bio cross-unit FRQs frequently demand chemiosmotic mastery.

questions.prompt: Catalytic ribonucleoprotein performing translation in all known life.
questions.explanation: Ribosome unifies Units 1 (rRNA + protein structure), 2 (free vs. ER-bound), 6 (translation), 7 (rRNA phylogeny: Woese 1977 three domains — Bacteria, Archaea, Eukarya). Ribozyme catalytic center (Steitz, Yonath, Ramakrishnan 2009 Nobel) supports RNA-world origin (Unit 7). 70S prokaryotic (target of streptomycin, tetracycline, erythromycin — basis of selective antibiotics; Unit 2/6/7). 80S eukaryotic. The peptidyl transferase reaction is rRNA-catalyzed — direct evidence ribosomes are ancient ribozymes. AP Bio cross-unit FRQs use ribosome diversity to test phylogenetics.

questions.prompt: Phospholipid bilayer protein that lets ions flow down their gradient with selectivity.
questions.explanation: Ion channels span Units 2 (membrane transport: K+, Na+, Ca2+ channels — MacKinnon 2003 Nobel for K+ channel structure), 3 (chemiosmotic H+ flow through ATP synthase rotor), 4 (signaling: ligand-gated nAChR, NMDA; voltage-gated channels in action potentials; SAC checkpoint Ca2+ release), 8 (osmoregulation in marine fish; aquaporin water channels — Agre 2003 Nobel). Patch clamp (Neher-Sakmann 1991 Nobel) records single channels. Hodgkin-Huxley action potential (1952 Nobel 1963). AP Bio integrates channel function across cell physiology + neurobiology.

questions.prompt: Evolutionary force decreasing genetic variation in small populations.
questions.explanation: Genetic drift unifies Units 5 (Mendelian alleles), 7 (population genetics — Wright 1931 mathematical theory), 8 (conservation: small populations lose diversity and accumulate deleterious alleles). Effect inversely proportional to effective population size (Ne). Founder effect + bottleneck effects amplify drift. Kimura's neutral theory (1968): most molecular substitutions are neutral and fixed by drift. Conservation biology: cheetah Ne ~7,000 with low diversity (bottleneck ~10,000 years ago); island fox populations crashed in 1990s and were rescued by captive breeding. Drift dominates selection when Ne·s < 1. AP Bio cross-unit FRQs ask students to predict drift effects.

questions.prompt: Three-tiered regulation: chromatin → transcription → post-transcriptional → translation.
questions.explanation: Multilayered gene regulation unifies Units 1 (chromatin structure — histone modification, DNA methylation), 4 (cell-cycle + signaling-driven transcription), 6 (TFs, splicing, miRNA, translation control), 7 (regulatory evolution — King and Wilson 1975 'humans and chimps share 99% protein sequence but differ in regulation'). Levels: chromatin remodeling (SWI/SNF, histone code — Allis-Jenuwein 2000), transcription (TFs + enhancers + Mediator), post-transcriptional (splicing, mRNA stability, miRNA targeting via RISC — Fire-Mello 2006 Nobel for RNAi), translation (eIF activity, uORFs), post-translation (phosphorylation, ubiquitination, localization). Cancer dysregulates at every level. AP Bio's biggest cross-unit synthesis topic.

questions.prompt: Meselson-Stahl design proving DNA replication is what mechanism?
questions.explanation: Meselson-Stahl (1958) — semiconservative replication — links Units 5 (chromosome behavior), 6 (DNA replication enzymes), 7 (universal mechanism across life supporting common ancestry). E. coli grown on 15N then shifted to 14N: after 1 generation, all DNA hybrid density (rules out conservative); after 2 gen, half hybrid + half light (rules out dispersive). Cited as 'the most beautiful experiment in biology.' AP Bio expects mastery of CsCl gradient interpretation, prediction of band patterns under each model, and recognition of WHY each generation's pattern excludes specific hypotheses — Science Practice 6 argumentation.

questions.prompt: Grant's Galapagos finch study documenting beak size selection in 1977 drought.
questions.explanation: Peter and Rosemary Grant's study of Geospiza fortis on Daphne Major (1973-present) — direct documentation of natural selection in real time. 1977 drought killed most small seeds → birds with larger, stronger beaks survived → next generation's mean beak depth rose 1.5 mm in ONE generation. Subsequent wet years reversed selection. Demonstrates heritability + directional selection + reversibility. Links Units 5 (heritable variation, h² of beak depth ~0.7), 7 (evolution in real time), 8 (selection by environment). AP Bio FRQs use the Grants' decadal data set as a canonical evolution-in-action case. Won 2009 Kyoto Prize.

questions.prompt: Avery-MacLeod-McCarty experiment identifying this as the genetic material.
questions.explanation: Avery, MacLeod, McCarty (1944) — extended Griffith's 1928 mouse pneumococcus transformation experiment. They purified components of heat-killed virulent S strain pneumococci and showed only DNA could transform avirulent R strain to virulent. Protein-degrading + RNA-degrading enzymes did NOT block transformation; DNase did. Yet some resisted: 'maybe a contaminating protein.' Hershey-Chase (1952) followed with bacteriophage T2: radiolabeled 35S-protein vs. 32P-DNA. Only 32P entered bacterial cells → DNA, not protein, carries genetic information. Watson-Crick structure (1953) followed within months. AP Bio Unit 5/6/7 demands deep familiarity with these experiments.

questions.prompt: Lipid messenger formed from PIP2 by phospholipase C; releases ER Ca2+.
questions.explanation: IP3 unifies Units 1 (phospholipid PIP2 structure), 4 (signal transduction: GPCR → PLC → cleaves PIP2 → IP3 + DAG; IP3 opens ER Ca2+ channels, Ca2+ acts as second messenger; DAG activates PKC), 6 (Ca2+ activates calmodulin → kinases → transcription factor phosphorylation), 8 (Ca2+ signaling in muscle contraction, neurotransmitter release). Berridge and Schulz discovered IP3 signaling (1980s). PIP3 (a different lipid from IP3) is generated by PI3K and activates Akt — frequently dysregulated in cancer. AP Bio cross-unit FRQs use IP3 to test integration of biochemistry + signaling + physiology.

questions.prompt: Sutherland's classic 1971 experiment on epinephrine identifying this 2nd messenger.
questions.explanation: Earl Sutherland (Nobel 1971) — second messengers — discovered cAMP via studies of epinephrine action on liver glycogen breakdown. Disrupted liver tissue still responded biochemically (broken cells but membranes intact); identified heat-stable, dialyzable cAMP as the intracellular signal. Adenylyl cyclase generates cAMP from ATP under Gs activation. cAMP activates PKA, which phosphorylates glycogen phosphorylase kinase → phosphorylase → glucose-1-phosphate from glycogen — Sutherland's full pathway elucidation. Cholera toxin locks Gs ON → constant cAMP → massive fluid secretion (the molecular basis of cholera's deadly diarrhea). AP Bio Unit 4 integrates Sutherland's pathway with signaling diagrams.

questions.prompt: Statistical formula testing observed vs. expected counts in genetics + population data.
questions.explanation: Chi-square (Pearson 1900) — χ² = Σ[(observed - expected)²/expected] — unifies Units 5 (Mendelian ratio testing: 3:1, 9:3:3:1) and 7 (Hardy-Weinberg deviation diagnosis). AP Bio exam provides χ² critical value table; students must compute χ², select correct df (categories - 1), and compare to critical value at α = 0.05 to accept or reject null hypothesis. Science Practice 5 question type. Linkage detection in dihybrid crosses (χ² significantly > critical → reject independent assortment → genes linked). HW equilibrium testing (deviation indicates evolutionary force acting). Common student mistakes: wrong df, wrong critical value, forgetting that χ² tests proportions.

questions.prompt: Hardy-Weinberg formula computing allele frequencies if 1% have recessive disease.
questions.explanation: If q² = 0.01 (1% affected with recessive disease), q = √0.01 = 0.1. p = 1 - 0.1 = 0.9. Carrier frequency 2pq = 2 × 0.9 × 0.1 = 0.18 = 18% of population. Homozygous dominant p² = 0.81. AP Bio cross-unit FRQ: given disease frequency, calculate carrier frequency (always far higher than affected frequency for recessive diseases). Real examples: cystic fibrosis q ≈ 0.02 in NW Europeans, 2pq ≈ 4% carriers; PKU q ≈ 0.01, 2pq ≈ 2% carriers; sickle-cell q ≈ 0.04 in West African ancestry. Science Practice 5 quantitative reasoning.

questions.prompt: Energy at 4th trophic level if producers fix 10,000 kJ/m²/yr by 10% rule.
questions.explanation: 10% rule cascade: producers 10,000 → primary consumer 1,000 → secondary consumer 100 → tertiary consumer 10 kJ/m²/yr. Lindeman 1942. Energy lost as heat (cellular respiration entropy), motion, undigested matter, dead biomass at each level. Real-world: ~10% is mean; range 1-25%. AP Bio cross-unit FRQ: students must compute and explain WHY only ~3-5 trophic levels exist (energy runs out). Also explains why eating lower on food chain is more energy-efficient (1 kg beef ~ 10 kg feed). Science Practice 5 quantitative reasoning unifying Units 3 + 8.

questions.prompt: ATP yield per glucose under aerobic respiration in liver cells with malate-aspartate shuttle.
questions.explanation: Aerobic respiration ATP yield depends on shuttle: liver uses malate-aspartate shuttle (preserves NADH equivalency → ~2.5 ATP per cytosolic NADH); skeletal muscle/brain uses glycerol-3-phosphate shuttle (converts to FADH2 → ~1.5 ATP per cytosolic NADH). Yields: 2 ATP (glycolysis substrate-level) + 2 ATP (TCA substrate-level) + ~28 ATP from chemiosmosis (10 NADH × 2.5 + 2 FADH2 × 1.5) = ~32 ATP in liver/heart. ~30 ATP in muscle/brain. Older textbooks said 36-38 (older P:O ratios). Anaerobic: 2 ATP. AP Bio Unit 3 quantitative reasoning. Even small wording matters: 'maximum' vs. 'typical' yield.

questions.prompt: Recombination frequency for two genes producing 200 recombinants of 1000 offspring.
questions.explanation: Recombination frequency (RF) = recombinant offspring / total offspring × 100% = (200/1000) × 100% = 20%. This corresponds to a map distance of 20 cM (centimorgans). Maximum possible RF is 50% (unlinked behavior). 1 cM ≈ 1 Mb DNA on average in humans (varies). Sturtevant (1913) built the first genetic map using RF data from Drosophila as a Morgan-lab undergraduate. Modern mapping uses SNP genotyping. AP Bio Unit 5 cross-unit quantitative reasoning. Three-point testcrosses can detect double crossovers and interference. Always present recombinants as MINORITY classes in offspring counts (parental classes more abundant if linked).

questions.prompt: Theme integrating Units 1, 2, 6: information stored, replicated, expressed.
questions.explanation: Information flow (central dogma) unifies storage (DNA helix — Unit 1, 6), replication (semiconservative — Unit 6), expression (transcription + translation — Unit 6), and regulation (multilayer — Units 4, 6). The flow is universal across life (Unit 7 evolution: same genetic code argues for LUCA). Variation in information (mutation, recombination) drives evolution (Unit 7). AP Bio enduring understanding IST: 'Information flow and storage from genome to organism' is a College Board Big Idea. Cross-unit FRQs frequently demand integration of these concepts.

questions.prompt: Theme integrating Units 2, 3, 4, 8: cells, organisms, ecosystems balance via this.
questions.explanation: Homeostasis (Bernard 1865 'milieu intérieur'; Cannon 1929 coined term) — maintenance of stable internal conditions via negative feedback — unifies cells (Unit 2 osmoregulation, pH), organisms (Unit 3/4: blood glucose by insulin/glucagon, body temp by hypothalamus, blood Ca by PTH-calcitonin, blood pH by bicarbonate buffer + respiration + kidney), populations (Unit 8 carrying capacity, predator-prey oscillations), ecosystems (Unit 8 biogeochemical cycles in balance). Negative feedback loops are the architectural core. Positive feedback (childbirth, action potential, blood clotting) is rarer but critical. AP Bio College Board Big Idea ENE: 'Living systems use information to acquire, store, and use energy.'.

questions.prompt: Theme integrating Units 5, 6, 7: heritable variation drives all evolution.
questions.explanation: Heritable variation (Mendel's units → Watson-Crick's DNA → modern molecular variation) unifies inheritance (Unit 5), molecular basis (Unit 6: mutation, crossover, recombination, mobile elements), and evolution (Unit 7: variation is required for natural selection — without it, no selection possible). Sources: point mutations (~10^-9/bp/gen), indels, transposable elements (~45% of human genome derived from transposons), CNVs, gene flow, polyploidy. Selection acts on PHENOTYPE but only HERITABLE phenotypic variation evolves. AP Bio College Board Big Idea EVO: 'The process of evolution drives the diversity and unity of life.' Cross-unit synthesis FRQs unify these scales.

questions.prompt: Theme integrating all 8 units: cells, organisms, and populations interact via this.
questions.explanation: Signaling unifies all scales: intracellular (Unit 4: kinase cascades, second messengers, transcription factor activation; Units 5/6 chromatin/transcriptional response), intercellular (Unit 4: paracrine, endocrine, autocrine, juxtacrine, synaptic), inter-organismal (Unit 8: pheromones, alarm calls, courtship displays, predator-prey signaling), inter-trophic (Unit 8: plant-pollinator signaling, host-parasite signaling), and ecosystem-scale (Unit 8: biogeochemical cycle feedbacks). AP Bio College Board Big Idea IST: 'Living systems store, retrieve, transmit, and respond to information essential to life processes.' Disruption causes disease (cancer signaling, hormonal disorders) or ecological collapse (pollinator loss).

questions.prompt: Theme integrating Units 1-8: ATP synthase across all life shows this evolutionary conservation.
questions.explanation: Common ancestry (Darwin 1859) unifies all biology. Evidence at every level: universal genetic code (Unit 6, 7); universal use of L-amino acids + D-sugars (Unit 1); ATP universal currency (Unit 3) and ATP synthase architecture conserved bacteria-to-humans; ribosomes universal across all three domains (Woese 1977); citric acid cycle universal in aerobes; HOX genes universal in animals (Lewis 1995 Nobel; same gene clusters pattern body axis from flies to humans); deep homology (Pax6 directs eye development in flies AND mice — Halder 1995). LUCA ~3.5-4 Bya. AP Bio College Board Big Idea EVO unifies all 8 units under common descent.
